title: REPL: inline wall and message notifications
description:
## Problem

The REPL has no push notification mechanism. In Claude Code, the status bar shows:
- Unread message count (bold yellow when > 0)
- Active wall broadcasts (bold red on line 2)
- Talk messages (bold yellow on line 2)

In the REPL, none of this exists. The user has to manually run `read` to discover messages and has zero visibility into wall broadcasts. This breaks the communication model — biff is supposed to be ambient awareness without context switching.

## Design

The REPL needs inline notifications that appear between commands or while the user is idle at the prompt. Two approaches to consider:

1. **Between-command notifications**: after each command completes, check for new messages/wall and print a notification line before the next prompt. Low complexity, but the user only sees notifications after pressing Enter.

2. **Async notifications**: use the NATS notification subscription (like talk does) to print notifications while the user is at the prompt. Higher complexity (need to interrupt the input line cleanly), but matches the MCP server's real-time behavior.

### What to show

- **New messages**: `[1 new message from @kai]` or similar indicator
- **Wall broadcasts**: `[WALL] @kai: release freeze (expires in 1h 45m)` when a wall is posted or changed
- **Wall clear**: `[WALL cleared]` when a wall expires or is cleared

### Constraints

- `input()` runs in `run_in_executor` — printing from the async side while the user is typing will interleave with their input. Need to handle this gracefully (e.g., clear line, print notification, reprint prompt).
- The heartbeat loop already runs in interactive mode, so we have a background task context.
- NATS subscriptions for talk notifications already exist in `_talk_loop` — the pattern is proven.
close_reason: Merged PR #112: REPL inline notifications, prompt gate, dispatch fixes

title: fortune: random quotes on the status bar (BSD vocabulary)
description:
## Overview

Resurrect BSD `fortune(6)` as a biff status bar feature. When no wall is active, the fortune line shows a random quote. Wall always takes priority (it's operational; fortune is ambient).

## How It Works

- Quotes stored in flat text files with entries delimited by `%` on a line by itself (BSD format)
- **Source the original BSD fortune database** — the classic `fortunes` file from 4.4BSD or FreeBSD is public domain. Use the real thing rather than curating from scratch.
- Ship default categories: `fortunes` (general), `literature`, `science`, `tech` — no `off` category (not the era)
- `fortune` tool picks a random entry and sets it as the status bar fortune
- Uses the same status bar line 2 as wall, but without bold red (neutral color or no color)
- Wall always overrides fortune (wall = operational urgency, fortune = ambient texture)
- Fortune rotates on session start or on demand via `/fortune`

## Status Bar Behavior

```text
Line 1: biff:main | 42% | $3.20 | jmf-pobox:tty1(2)
Line 2: (wall active)   → WALL: release freeze              (bold red)
         (no wall)       → The best way to predict the future is to invent it.  (neutral)
         (no wall, no fortune) → (no line 2)
```

## Data Format (BSD-compatible)

```text
The best way to predict the future is to invent it.
    — Alan Kay
%
Any sufficiently advanced technology is indistinguishable from magic.
    — Arthur C. Clarke
%
```

## Scope

- `/fortune` command to rotate the quote manually
- `fortune` MCP tool for programmatic access
- Source original BSD fortune files (4.4BSD/FreeBSD, public domain)
- Ship 2-3 curated category files (tech, literature, general)
- Status line integration: fortune on line 2 when no wall active
- No `strfile`/`.dat` optimization needed — files are small enough to read directly

title: Voice talk sessions via punt-tts
description:
Speak incoming /talk messages in real-time, making talk sessions feel like voice conversations. Each message is spoken as it arrives — hands-free real-time coordination while coding.

## Projection Model

Talk messages arrive in the background poller (Python, asyncio). This is the strongest case for the library projection — latency matters for real-time conversation feel. Shelling out to `tts speak` adds process spawn overhead on every message. TTSClient.synthesize() in-process is faster.

However, if biff-k8u ships CLI-first for v1, talk can use CLI too. The messages are short (1-2 sentences) and arrive at human typing speed, so the shell overhead may be acceptable. Library import for v2 if latency is noticeable.

## Behavior

- When in an active /talk session, each incoming message is spoken: 'kai says: sure, looking now.'
- Per-sender voices from .biff config so each person is recognizable by ear.
- Conversational tone, slightly faster rate — these are short real-time exchanges.
- Agent-to-agent /talk produces audible dialogue the human can overhear. Two distinct voices debating or coordinating.
- Respect /mesg n — no audio when DND.
- Respect voice_enabled — no audio when voice is off.

## Implementation Path (CLI v1)

1. In talk_listen message handler, check `which tts` and voice_enabled
2. Shell out: `tts speak --text "kai says: $MESSAGE" --voice $SENDER_VOICE --ephemeral`
3. Non-blocking (fire and forget) — don't wait for speech to finish before accepting next message

## Implementation Path (Library v2)

1. `from punt_tts.core import TTSClient` (optional import)
2. Synthesize directly with per-sender voice
3. Enqueue via punt_tts.playback.enqueue() for flock-serialized playback

Depends on: biff-k8u (shared voice detection, per-sender voice config)
close_reason: Duplicate of biff-ur2 (talk voice via vox). Richer description merged.

title: /mesg command — send async message
description:
Slash command: /mesg @user "text"
Maps to MCP tool: mcp__biff__send_message
Arguments: to (user), message (text)
Behavior: Send a one-way async message. Recipient reads when they choose (POP semantics).
Also surfaces check_messages on the receiving end.
Unix ancestor: mesg (BSD)
PR/FAQ: Phase 1 Must Have

title: Support teleport and web sessions
description:
Make biff work across session teleportation and web-hosted Claude Code sessions (https://code.claude.com/docs/en/claude-code-on-the-web).

## Context

Claude Code sessions can move between environments:
- **Teleport** (`/teleport`, `/tp`): pull a web session down to the local terminal, preserving conversation history and branch
- **Web sessions** (`&` prefix): start a task on cloud infrastructure, monitor from browser
- Sessions cross the boundary between local CLI and cloud-hosted execution

## Challenges for biff

- **Status line**: runs as a local CLI command. Web sessions have different UI — status line may not exist in cloud context.
- **Session identity**: a teleported session changes host and potentially TTY. Biff session (user:tty on host) needs to survive or gracefully hand off.
- **MCP server**: locally spawned via stdio. In web context, the MCP server lifecycle may differ.
- **Unread state**: keyed by PPID in `~/.biff/unread/`. Web sessions have different process trees.
- **Presence**: `/who` shows host+dir. A teleported session changes host mid-conversation.

## Open questions

- Does the web runner have access to `biff` CLI? (probably not without explicit setup)
- Can biff detect it is running in a web/cloud context?
- Should teleportation trigger a logout+login event in `/last`?
- Is the web session a new biff session or a continuation of the local one?

## Depends on

- biff-2n1: CLI duality (web sessions may only have CLI access, not MCP)

title: Fix: fire notifications/tools/list_changed on description mutation
description:
The check_messages tool description is mutated when unread messages arrive, but notifications/tools/list_changed is never sent to Claude Code. This means Claude never re-reads the tool list and never sees the updated description.

Two paths need fixing:
1. Belt (after tool calls): refresh_check_messages runs inside a tool handler where Context is available. Queue the notification via context._queue_tool_list_changed().
2. Suspenders (background poll): poll_inbox runs in a background asyncio.Task outside any request context. Needs direct session access to call session.send_tool_list_changed().

The PR/FAQ (line 480) says this was validated by spike, but the current implementation silently mutates without notifying.

Files: src/biff/server/tools/_descriptions.py, possibly src/biff/server/app.py
close_reason: Fixed in PR #21 — notifications/tools/list_changed now fires on description mutation

title: REPL talk: two-phase handshake — wait for accept before entering talk mode
description:
## Problem

The talk inviter enters talk mode immediately after sending the invitation.
In BSD talk, the inviter waited with "Waiting for your party to respond"
until the other side accepted. The current behavior means the inviter
is typing into talk mode before the target even sees the invitation.

## Design

Two-phase handshake:
1. Inviter sends invitation notification, enters waiting state
   ("Waiting for @user:tty to respond...")
2. Target sees banner, types talk @inviter:tty to accept
3. Target entering talk sends an "accept" notification back
4. Inviter receives accept, enters talk mode
5. If inviter types "end" during waiting, cancel and return to REPL
6. Timeout after N seconds returns to REPL with "No response"

The accept notification uses the same NATS core pub/sub as the invitation.
The waiting state reuses the _wait_for_input_or_notify pattern.
close_reason: Merged PR #114: two-phase talk handshake, Z specs, 1075 tests

title: Audio notifications via vox CLI — status line workaround
description:
## Motivation

DES-022 proved that biff's NATS delivery and unread file writes work correctly, but Claude Code stops polling `biff statusline` during idle periods. The status bar — biff's only visual notification channel — is blind between tool calls. Audio bypasses this entirely.

## Constraint

**Biff MCP server → tts CLI.** No library import, no `punt-biff[voice]` extra, no `from punt_tts.core import TTSClient`. Shell out to `tts speak --ephemeral` as a subprocess. This keeps biff and tts loosely coupled — if tts isn't installed, biff works exactly as before.

## Integration Points

Two NATS callbacks in `_descriptions.py` receive messages in real-time:

1. `_on_talk_msg` — incoming talk messages (sender, body, from_key)
2. Wall receipt in the background poller — wall broadcasts

Both have the message content and can fire a subprocess.

## Design Questions

- Where exactly to call `tts speak` (NATS callback vs poller tick)
- Voice selection (per-sender from .biff config, or default)
- Rate limiting (skip if previous tts still running?)
- DND respect (mesg n suppresses audio)
- voice_enabled check (.tts/config.md)
- Deduplication (don't re-speak on poller re-reads)

## Relationship to Existing Beads

Supersedes the implementation path in biff-k8u (library projection removed). biff-0ps and biff-5et remain valid as the talk and wall sub-features. biff-nqc (assessment) is effectively answered by DES-022.

title: suppress-output.sh counts lines instead of messages
description: PostToolUse hooks in suppress-output.sh use wc -l to count output rows, but 80-char line wrapping means a single message spanning multiple lines inflates the count. /read showed '7 new' for 1 message wrapped across 7 lines. Fix: change COUNT=$(printf '%s' "$RESULT" | wc -l | tr -d ' ') to count actual data rows (e.g. grep -c '^   @') on lines 31, 54, 77.
close_reason: Merged PR #116: suppress-output.sh counts data rows not wrapped lines

title: Race condition in TTY name auto-assignment allows duplicate ttyN names
close_reason: Merged PR #117: TTY name race condition fixed via write-then-verify

title: Talk status bar latency: tool description mutation parity with wall
description: Talk messages appear in the unread file but not on the status bar until the next Claude Code poll. Root cause: notify_tool_list_changed() fires but Claude Code sees no tool description change, so no UI re-render triggers. Wall works because refresh_wall() mutates the wall tool description. Fix: add refresh_talk() that mirrors refresh_wall() — mutates the talk tool description to [TALK] @sender: message when messages arrive. DES-020.
close_reason: Fixed in v0.10.6 — PR #78. Added refresh_talk() mirroring refresh_wall(). DES-020 settled.

title: Speak /read inbox messages aloud via vox
description: When the user runs /read to check their inbox and voice is enabled, speak each unread message aloud via vox. This turns inbox review into an audio experience — like listening to voicemail. Each message spoken with the sender's voice if known. Could be sequential (one by one) or batched via vox ensemble.

title: Document restart requirement after namespace-changing upgrades
description:
## Problem

After PR #39 (slug-based NATS namespace), servers started before the change remain on the old namespace (`biff-biff-inbox`) while new servers use the new namespace (`biff-punt-labs__biff-inbox`). Messages don't cross namespaces. Users see split-brain behavior until all sessions are restarted.

This is inherent to long-running MCP servers that load config at startup. Any config change that affects the NATS namespace (repo_name) requires a full restart of all biff servers.

## Fix

1. Add a note to CHANGELOG/README about restart requirements after upgrade
2. Consider: `biff doctor` could detect namespace mismatches (compare running server's repo_name to what load_config would produce now)
3. Consider: version check — server could compare its loaded version against the installed package version and warn if stale

## Acceptance

- `biff doctor` warns if running servers might be on an old namespace
- Or: documented in upgrade instructions
close_reason: DES-016 moved to shared streams with idempotent upsert provisioning. Namespace mismatches are no longer a practical concern — stream_prefix is fixed at 'biff' in production and config changes are rare.

title: Spike: multi-agent worktree coordination proof-of-concept
description:
## Goal

Time-boxed proof-of-concept that validates the multi-agent coordination design end-to-end. Two agents in the same directory on the same machine detect the conflict, coordinate via /write, and create worktrees — all using biff's communication primitives.

## What We're Proving

1. **TTY identity works** (biff-kds subset): Two MCP server instances in the same repo register as distinct sessions with unique TTY IDs. /who shows them separately.

2. **Host+dir presence works** (biff-roa subset): Each session publishes hostname and pwd. /who displays these columns and flags same-host+dir overlap.

3. **Targeted /write works** (biff-kds subset): An agent can /write to a specific TTY, not just a username. The recipient gets a push notification (tool name change mechanism).

4. **The coordination flow works** (biff-bf8 preview): Agent claims bead → checks /who → sees collision → creates worktree → /write other agent → other agent acts on notification.

## Key Design Insight: TTY from Connection Port

Each `biff serve` process connects to NATS with a unique ephemeral source port assigned by the OS. This port serves as the natural TTY identifier:

- **No generation needed** — the OS assigns it, like Unix PTY allocation (`/dev/pts/3`)
- **Unique per process** — no collision risk
- **Stable for connection lifetime** — consistent identity for the session
- **Available via NATS client API** — `nc.client_id` or underlying socket port

Session key changes from `{user}` to `{user}:{port}` in NATS KV. The optional `/tty <name>` (from biff-kds) becomes a human-friendly label on top of the natural ID.

Current architecture gap: `UserSession` (models.py:66) is keyed by `user` only — two sessions for the same user stomp each other's presence. The spike fixes this by adding the TTY dimension.

## What We're NOT Building

- Full AGENTS.md injection (/biff y|n) — spike uses manual agent instructions
- Wall duration/status line enhancements — spike uses basic /wall
- Plan auto-expand — spike uses manual plan text
- Full TTY lifecycle (naming, listing, cleanup) — spike uses raw port IDs
- Server-side collision detection — spike relies on client reading /who output

## Spike Scope

### Minimum changes:

**Models:**
- Add `tty: str` field to `UserSession` (default: auto-assigned from connection)
- Add `hostname: str` and `pwd: str` fields to `UserSession`

**Relay (NATS):**
- Session KV key becomes `{user}:{tty}` instead of `{user}`
- /who returns all sessions (multiple per user), including host+dir columns
- /write accepts `user:tty` syntax for targeted delivery
- /write to bare `user` fans out to all sessions for that user

**Server startup:**
- Read NATS client connection port (or client ID) as TTY identifier
- Read `socket.gethostname()` and `os.getcwd()` for presence
- Publish enriched session on connect

**Tests:**
- Integration test: two clients in same tmp_path, different TTYs
- Both register presence with same hostname+pwd
- Client A calls /who, sees both sessions with host+dir
- Client A /writes to Client B's specific TTY
- Client B receives the message (not Client A)
- /write to bare username delivers to both

## Success Criteria

- [ ] Two sessions for same user appear as distinct entries in /who
- [ ] /who output includes hostname and directory per session
- [ ] /write @user:tty delivers to specific session only
- [ ] /write @user delivers to all sessions for that user
- [ ] Push notification reaches the targeted session
- [ ] Old single-session behavior still works (backwards compatible)

## Related Beads

This spike de-risks and informs:
- biff-kds (TTY sessions) — validates the connection-port-as-TTY model
- biff-roa (enriched presence) — validates host+dir in presence
- biff-bf8 (AGENTS.md + hooks) — validates the coordination flow
- biff-klz (wall) — unrelated to spike but benefits from TTY foundation
- biff-5zq (plan auto-expand) — unrelated to spike but benefits from presence enrichment
close_reason: Spike complete: TTY sessions proven with 386 tests passing

title: Adopt punt-kit Projection Model
description:
Adopt punt-kit Projection Model: every project is a library first, projected outward as CLI/MCP/plugin. Biff's architecture already follows the pattern internally — core modules, thin MCP frontend. This bead completes the projection by exposing the library API and adding the CLI projection.

## Three deliverables

### 1. Library API — `__init__.py` with `__all__`

The only missing piece. A downstream Python app cannot `from biff import ...` and do useful work today.

```python
# biff/__init__.py — the public library API
from biff.config import load_config, Config
from biff.models import UserSession, Message, SessionEvent
from biff.nats_relay import NatsRelay
from biff.relay import Relay

__all__ = [
    "Config",
    "Message",
    "NatsRelay",
    "Relay",
    "SessionEvent",
    "UserSession",
    "load_config",
]
```

Reference: quarry `__init__.py` (gold standard).

### 2. CLI duality — all commands as CLI subcommands

Every biff operation usable from the terminal as `biff <command>`, not just via MCP. Follows quarry pattern: shared core, thin CLI + MCP frontends.

| CLI Command | MCP Tool | Core Function |
|-------------|----------|---------------|
| `biff write @user "msg"` | `write` | `relay.deliver()` |
| `biff read` | `read_messages` | `relay.fetch()` + `relay.mark_read()` |
| `biff who` | `who` | `relay.get_sessions()` |
| `biff finger @user` | `finger` | `relay.get_sessions()` filtered |
| `biff plan "msg"` | `plan` | `relay.update_session()` |
| `biff mesg on/off` | `mesg` | `relay.update_session()` |
| `biff last` | `last` | `relay.get_wtmp()` |

CLI commands use ephemeral relay connections (connect → act → disconnect) without claiming a session slot.

### 3. `--json` global flag

Machine-readable output per punt-kit CLI standard (cli.md §Machine-Readable Output).

- Global flag on typer app, not per-subcommand
- When set: all stdout is valid JSON, human-readable messages go to stderr
- Errors: `{"error": "<message>"}`
- List commands return arrays; detail commands return objects
- Pattern: `bd --json list`, `bd --json show <id>`

## What this is NOT

- No `BiffClient` wrapper class. The Relay Protocol IS the library API.
- No refactoring of MCP server. It already follows the thin-frontend pattern.

## References

- [Projection Model](../punt-kit/standards/distribution.md)
- [Package Architecture](../punt-kit/standards/python.md) — `__init__.py`, quarry gold standard
- [CLI Standards](../punt-kit/standards/cli.md) — duality, --json, required subcommands
close_reason: All three deliverables shipped in PR #98: library API (__init__.py with __all__), CLI duality (all commands as subcommands), --json global flag. Verified working.

title: NATS resource optimization: streams, consumers, and config
description:
## Problem

NATS resource configuration is not fully understood or optimized. Hit "maximum consumers limit reached" (error 10026) during testing. The demo account allows 20 consumers per stream — with multiple sessions and duplicate biff plugins, this limit can be reached.

## Resolved by DES-015 (PR #61)

- Consumer limit is no longer the bottleneck. Steady-state consumer footprint is 0 per user.
- get_unread_summary() uses stream_info() metadata only — zero consumers created.
- fetch()/fetch_user_inbox() delete consumers in try/finally — cleanup on error paths.
- stream_info x2 per poll cycle remains but is metadata-only (no consumers).

## Remaining Issues

1. **No max_age on inbox stream** — unread messages sit forever until 10 MiB fills. Should expire after 7-30 days.
2. **No explicit max_consumers on streams** — relying on account defaults. Less critical now with 0 steady-state consumers.
3. **Duplicate biff plugins in biff repo** — biff@punt-labs and biff-dev both start biff serve, doubling NATS connections.
4. **Stream count approaching account limit** — 3 streams per repo × 25 limit = 8 repos max. See biff-wg4 (DES-016).
5. **Stale sessions in KV** — 3-day TTL means crashed sessions show in /who for up to 3 days.

## Key Files

- src/biff/nats_relay.py — all resource definitions and consumer configs
- src/biff/server/app.py — session lifecycle, heartbeat, sentinel reaper
- src/biff/relay.py — SESSION_TTL_SECONDS (3 days)
- DESIGN.md — DES-006 through DES-015 for architectural context
close_reason: DES-015 fixed consumer limits (0 steady-state consumers), DES-016 fixed stream limits (3 shared streams). Remaining minor items (max_age, stale session TTL) can be refiled independently if needed.

title: Include TTY name in status line output
description:
## Problem

The status line currently shows `biff(N)` or `project(N)` — repo-scoped only. With multiple sessions open for the same repo, there's no way to tell WHICH session you're looking at from the status bar.

## Fix

Include the TTY name (from `/tty`) in the status line segment. E.g. `biff(1) tty1` or `biff:tty1(1)`.

## Feasibility question

The status line script (`biff statusline`) runs as a standalone shell command — it reads `~/.biff/unread/*.json` files but has no connection to the MCP server. It doesn't know which session it belongs to.

Options:
1. **MCP server writes TTY name to the unread file** — extend `unread.json` to include `{"count": N, "preview": "...", "tty_name": "tty1"}`. But multiple servers write to the same file (repo-scoped), stomping each other's TTY name.
2. **Per-TTY unread files** — `~/.biff/unread/{repo}-{tty}.json`. Status line sums counts across files for the same repo. Each server writes only its own file. Requires the status line to group by repo.
3. **Env var from Claude Code** — Claude Code passes session identity to the status line command via stdin JSON. The status line could use this to look up the TTY name from `sessions.json`. Depends on what Claude Code exposes.

Option 2 aligns with biff-bej (TTY-scoped status bar) and solves both problems.

## Depends on

- biff-bej (TTY-scoped status bar) — likely implemented together

## Acceptance

- Each Claude Code session's status bar shows its own TTY name
- Two sessions for the same repo show different TTY names
close_reason: Resolved by PPID statusline PR #40: mesg is per-session (user:tty key), TTY name shown in status line

title: Provisioning service: per-repo NATS credentials via GitHub identity
description:
Replace bundled shared demo credentials with per-repo NATS JWTs provisioned via GitHub identity.

## Approach

Piggyback on existing `gh` CLI authentication rather than building a full OAuth flow:

1. `biff init` (or `biff auth`) calls `gh api user` to get GitHub token
2. Resolves the current repo via `git remote` (e.g. `punt-labs/biff`)
3. POST to a biff provisioning service with the token + repo identifier
4. Service verifies token, checks the user has access to that repo
5. Provisions a NATS user JWT scoped to that repo's subject namespace
6. Returns credentials, stored in git config or local file

## Scoping model

- One NATS JWT per GitHub repo (not per user)
- Subject permissions: `biff.{org}.{repo}.>` — repos can't cross-talk
- All team members on a repo share the same credential
- Credential stored in `.biff` or `git config` (repo-local)

## Provisioning Service

Lightweight — Cloudflare Worker or Lambda. Responsibilities:
- Verify GitHub token authenticity
- Confirm caller has access to the claimed repo (GitHub API)
- Mint NATS user JWTs with subject permissions scoped to that repo
- Per-repo connection and data limits
- Revocation support

## Why

Bundled demo creds (biff-mcp 0.1.0) are fine for onboarding but shared across ALL repos — no isolation, abuse burns everyone's connection budget. Per-repo provisioning gives each team their own namespace and limits.

## Depends on

Demo relay confidence (bundled creds working reliably first).

title: Hook: load wall state at SessionStart
description:
Z spec audit finding: StartSession operation requires loading active wall state and injecting it as additionalContext. 

## Current State
- session-start.sh dispatches to hook.py handle_session_start()
- handle_session_start() has collision detection (good) but NO wall loading
- Wall state exists in relay (WallPost with TTL), but is never read at startup

## Required (from Z spec lines 287-323)
- Check relay for active wall post
- If wall exists: inject wall text into additionalContext
- Maps to StartSession inputs: hasWall? = ztrue, wallContent? = {text}

## Implementation
In handle_session_start() (hook.py ~line 448):
1. Query relay for current wall: relay.get_wall() or similar
2. If wall exists and not expired, append to parts[]
3. Format: 'Active wall: {wall_text}'

## Validated by
Z spec invariant: wallActive = ztrue at StartSession => context includes wall text
UC6 (Broadcast) and UC15 (CI/CD Status) depend on this for wall visibility.
close_reason: Wall marker in markers.py, wall tool writes/clears it, session_start reads and injects active wall text

title: Docs: document git hook + hint file architecture for plan auto-detect
description:
Z spec audit finding: The plan auto-detect mechanism is architecturally different from what the Z spec models, but functionally correct. Worth documenting.

## Current State (correct, needs documentation)
The Z spec models SetPlanAuto as a single PostToolUse Bash operation. The actual implementation splits this across two layers:

1. Git post-checkout hook writes ~/.biff/hints/plan-hint with branch name
2. PostToolUse Bash hook (check_plan_hint in hook.py ~line 278) reads and deletes the hint file
3. Returns guidance to agent to call /plan with the branch

This two-phase approach is architecturally sound:
- Git hooks fire reliably on checkout (independent of Claude Code)
- PostToolUse reads the hint on next Bash command (lazy evaluation)
- Hint file is consumed once (no stale state)

## Required
Document this architecture in DESIGN.md or the Z spec narrative so future contributors understand why the implementation differs from the spec's single-operation model.

## Same pattern used for
- Wall hints (git pre-push → check_wall_hint)
- Both use ~/.biff/hints/ directory
close_reason: Documented hint file and marker file architecture in DESIGN.md with tables, lifecycle, and Z spec relationship

title: Phase 1: Core MCP server
description:
Implement the foundational biff commands as an MCP server.

Single-user value from /plan and session awareness.

title: REPL: timestamps toggle for talk and read output
description:
## Problem

Talk messages show timestamps by default, which is noisy in a fast conversation. The REPL has no way to toggle timestamps on or off.

## Design

Add a `timestamps` toggle to the REPL:
- `timestamps on` / `timestamps off` — toggle inline timestamp display
- Default: off (cleaner output for interactive use)
- Affects: talk message display, read output, and any future notification output

title: Voice wall broadcasts via punt-tts
description:
When a /wall broadcast arrives, speak it aloud for urgency. Walls are rare and high-signal — the audio cost is negligible and the urgency justifies interruption.

## Projection Model

Wall display already flows through biff's shell scripts (status-line.sh renders the WALL line). The CLI projection is the natural fit: add `tts speak --text '...' --ephemeral` to the wall rendering path. No Python code change needed for the initial implementation.

If biff-k8u establishes the library projection for Python server code, wall broadcasts received by the background poller could also be spoken via TTSClient directly — but the shell path is simpler and sufficient for v1.

## Behavior

- On wall receipt, speak: 'Wall from eric: release freeze — do not push to main.'
- Distinct tone from regular messages — vibe tags like [alert] [serious] or [authoritative]
- Speak once on first receipt. Track last-spoken wall ID to prevent re-speaking on subsequent status bar polls.
- Respect /mesg n (DND) — no audio when messages are off.
- Respect voice_enabled in .tts/config.md — no audio when voice is off.

## Implementation Path

1. In the shell path that renders wall broadcasts, check `which tts` and voice_enabled
2. If both present, `tts speak --text "Wall from $SENDER: $MESSAGE" --ephemeral`
3. Track wall ID in a state file to prevent repeat announcements
4. Wall voice could be a fixed authoritative voice (e.g. 'roger') distinct from per-sender voices

Depends on: biff-k8u (shared voice detection and config reading)
close_reason: Duplicate of biff-a4d (wall voice via vox). Richer description merged.

title: Support Claude agent teams
description:
Make biff the visibility and coordination layer for Claude Code agent teams.

## Use Cases

Directly supports four use cases from the coordination foundation (docs/biff_coordination_use_cases_v0.2.md):

- **UC7** — Escalate a Decision to a Human: Agent /writes to human when it hits an ambiguous choice
- **UC8** — Report Task Completion: Agent /writes and /walls when PR is ready
- **UC11** — Avoid Duplicate Work: Agent checks /who before claiming a task
- **UC12** — Coordinate File Ownership: Agents /write to negotiate who works on what

## Key Insight: Hooks First, Not Detection

The original design assumed we needed to detect agent teams and build special support. The use case analysis shows the opposite: **all four agent coordination use cases work with existing commands today**. The gap is behavioral — agents don't know to use /who before claiming work, or to /write when they hit a decision point.

The implementation path is **T1 (hooks)**, not T3 (development):

1. **SessionStart hook** — when collision detection finds multiple sessions in the same repo, inject: "Other sessions are active. Run /who to check what others are working on. Set /plan before beginning to avoid duplicate effort."
2. **Stop hook** — when the agent finishes a turn with unresolved decisions, inject: "You identified an unresolved decision. Consider escalating via /write @human:tty."
3. **PostToolUse hook on PR creation** — already suggests /wall. Extend to suggest /write @human with the PR link.
4. **TeammateIdle hook** — natural moment for agent to check /read for redirections.

## Context

Agent teams (experimental) are independent Claude Code sessions coordinating via a shared task list and mailbox. Each teammate is a full Claude Code instance that loads CLAUDE.md, MCP servers, and plugins — including biff if installed. The built-in coordination is agent-to-agent; the human has limited visibility.

## What biff adds

**Single-machine visibility (the immediate win):**
- /who shows all agents across all teams from any terminal
- /finger @teammate shows what a specific agent is working on
- /plan auto-updates when agents claim beads, giving real-time status
- The human can /write to any agent to redirect or prioritize

**Cross-machine visibility (the NATS win):**
- Agent teams are local to one machine. Biff sees humans AND agents across machines.
- A developer running teams on laptop + cloud can see everything in one /who
- Prevents duplicate work across teams that don't know about each other (UC11)

**Persistence (the durability win):**
- Agent team state is ephemeral — cleaned up when the team ends
- Biff presence and messages persist across sessions
- /last shows historical agent activity after teams are gone

## Agent notification pathway

Two mechanisms deliver message awareness to agents:

**1. tools/list_changed (push):** Biff's poller fires tools/list_changed when messages arrive. Claude Code re-fetches tool descriptions, which include unread counts. The model sees updated descriptions on the next tool-list refresh.

**2. Hooks (turn-boundary injection):** Stop hooks and TeammateIdle hooks inject additionalContext when the agent is between turns. The hook checks for unread biff messages and tells the agent to call read_messages.

## Success Criteria

A human with two agent teams running can run /who from a third terminal and see all agents with their plans. The human can /write to any agent and the agent reads the message at its next turn boundary. Agents check /who before claiming work and /write to negotiate file ownership when they detect shared worktrees.

title: GitHub Actions CI notifications via biff (UC15)
description:
Add biff wall and biff write steps to GitHub Actions workflows so CI status changes notify the team in real-time.

## Use Case Reference

Implements UC15 (Notify Team of CI/CD Status) from docs/biff_coordination_use_cases_v0.2.md. Implementation tier: T2 (configuration only, no biff code changes).

2. **Workflow steps:** Add to .github/workflows/*.yml:
   - On failure: biff wall "CI: build failed on <branch> — <actor>" --duration 2h
   - On failure: biff write @<actor> "Your push broke CI: <link>"
   - On success (optional): short-TTL wall or skip

3. **Identity configuration:** Environment variables or .biff config that identifies the CI runner as github-actions user. Session registers on workflow start, expires on completion.

## No biff code changes required

The CLI already supports all commands. This is purely credential provisioning + workflow YAML configuration.

## Basic Course (from UC15)

1. GitHub Action step invokes biff wall with failure message and 2h duration
2. GitHub Action step invokes biff write @committer with targeted notification
3. System stores wall post and targeted message
4. All active sessions see CI status on status bar
5. Committer receives targeted push notification

title: Spike: MCP notification rendering and subprocess handoff
description:
Validate two critical feasibility questions before committing to the full Phase 1 implementation.

**Question 1: MCP notifications for receiving messages**
Can an MCP server push a notification to Claude Code that renders visibly in the terminal? Test:
- Create a minimal MCP server that fires a notification after a delay
- Verify it appears in the Claude Code session without a tool call
- Assess rendering quality (plain text? formatted? inline with conversation?)

**Question 2: Subprocess handoff for /talk**
Can an MCP server spawn an interactive subprocess that takes over terminal I/O, then return control to Claude Code cleanly? Test:
- MCP tool call spawns a simple interactive process (e.g., a chat loop)
- User types directly to the subprocess (no LLM intermediation)
- Process exits, Claude Code resumes normally
- Assess the UX transition (seamless or jarring?)

**Exit criteria:**
- Clear yes/no on both questions with evidence
- If notifications work: document the API and rendering constraints
- If subprocess handoff works: document the mechanism and UX quality
- If either fails: document alternatives and revised approach for /mesg and /talk

**Timeframe:** 1-2 days
notes:
Spike results (2026-02-13):

Q1 (MCP notifications render?): NO - notifications/message silently dropped by Claude Code (issue #3174, closed not planned).
Q2 (Subprocess handoff?): NO - stdio transport reserves stdout for JSON-RPC, no PTY allocation.

ALTERNATIVE DISCOVERED: HTTP transport + tools/list_changed
- FastMCP HTTP transport works (Streamable HTTP + SSE)
- Dynamic tool descriptions update via remove_tool/re-register
- notifications/tools/list_changed automatically sent on SSE channel
- Server-side validated via curl (all 5 checks pass)
- Same pattern used by Slack MCP server in production
- Claude Code end-to-end test pending (needs new session)

RECOMMENDATION: Proceed with HTTP transport architecture for Phase 1.
Architecture documented in docs/prd/biff-phase1-communication.md and docs/sparc/phase1-implementation.md
close_reason: Server-side validation complete. All 5 checks pass. HTTP transport + tools/list_changed is viable. Architecture recommendation: proceed with HTTP transport. Claude Code e2e test deferred to new session.

description:

## Near-Term Path (T2 — no code changes)

Before full OAuth, a simpler service token approach enables UC15:
1. Provision a NATS credential scoped to the repo's biff namespace

3. CI steps invoke biff wall and biff write via CLI
4. GitHub registers as github-actions:ttyN session

This requires only configuration (NATS token + workflow YAML), not biff code changes. The CLI already supports all the commands GitHub Actions needs.

## Full Path (T3 — future)

GitHub OAuth token auth with relay verification. Depends on encryption layer being in place.

Future scope — depends on encryption layer being in place.

title: Status bar display: bare repo name and neutral zero-count label
description:
## Problem

Two display issues in the status bar:

1. **Slug names are too long**: `punt-labs__biff` truncates to `punt-labs__…` at the 12-char limit. The slug (owner__repo) is great for NATS scoping but bad for human display.

2. **Default zero-count label is confusing**: When no projects have unread messages, `_biff_segment_multi` returns `biff(0)`. This looks like a project named 'biff' with 0 unread. Users see alternation between `punt-labs__…(1)` and `biff(0)` and think it's a bug.

## Fix

1. **Display name**: Use the bare repo name (last component of slug) for the status bar. `punt-labs__biff` → `biff`. Only use the full slug if two projects share the same bare name (collision, unlikely in practice).

2. **Zero-count label**: Change from `biff(0)` to just `biff` (no count, no parens) or an empty string. The absence of a count IS the signal. Bold yellow only when count > 0.

## Files

- `src/biff/statusline.py`: `_biff_segment_multi` and `ProjectUnread`
- Tests: `tests/test_statusline.py`

## Acceptance

- `punt-labs__biff` displays as `biff(1)` not `punt-labs__…(1)`
- Zero unread shows `biff` (plain) not `biff(0)`
close_reason: Closed

title: Phase 3: Real-time conversation, pairing, and security
description:
## /talk — Real-Time Human-Agent Conversation

BSD `talk(1)` opened a split terminal between two users. Both typed, both saw output in real-time. The session lasted until someone quit.

Biff `/talk` opens a message channel between two sessions (human-to-human, human-to-agent, or agent-to-agent). The receiving side enters a **listen loop** — its job is receiving and responding to messages until the talk ends.

### Motivating Scenario

You are working on biff. You go to dinner. You have a terminal on your phone (or a native iOS biff client). You "login" and see `/who` shows your agent is idle or stuck on a permission prompt. You want to say: "do bead biff-0ap next." Today you cannot. With `/talk`, you can.

### The `talk_listen` Tool — Long-Poll via NATS

The agent cannot sleep or poll. But the MCP server can **hold a tool call open** while awaiting a NATS message. Long-polling at the MCP tool level:

```text
Agent calls: talk_listen(timeout=30)
  |
MCP server awaits NATS subscription
  | (message arrives from @jmf-pobox)
Returns: "@jmf-pobox: do bead biff-0ap"
  |
Agent acts on message, responds via talk_reply
  |
Agent calls: talk_listen(timeout=30)  <- loop continues
```

No busy-loop. No polling. NATS is the transport, MCP long-poll is the bridge. The agent stays in the loop naturally because each `talk_listen` return gives it something to process. Timeout returns "no messages" and the agent re-calls.

### Talk Flow (Phone to Agent)

```text
Phone terminal:
  $ biff who
  @jmf-pobox  main   0m   +  m2-mb-air  biff  biff-xtc: read formatting

  $ biff talk jmf-pobox
  Connected to @jmf-pobox on main (biff)

  > do bead biff-0ap next
  < Starting biff-0ap: fortune feature.

  > use the freebsd fortune file
  < Found it in the FreeBSD ports tree. Parsing now.

  > talk end
  Talk ended. @jmf-pobox continuing autonomously.
```

### Agent Side Protocol

1. Talk request arrives via NATS, MCP server updates tool descriptions
2. Notification hook injects: "Talk request from @jmf-pobox. Call talk_accept."
3. Agent calls `talk_accept(user="jmf-pobox")`
4. Agent enters talk loop:
   - `result = talk_listen(timeout=30)`
   - If message: process it (question, instruction, approval), respond via `talk_reply`
   - If "talk end": exit loop, resume previous work
   - If timeout: re-listen
5. Talk ends, agent resumes work or picks from `bd ready`

### Tools

| Tool | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `talk` | Initiate a talk request to a session |
| `talk_accept` | Accept an incoming talk request |
| `talk_listen` | Block until message arrives (long-poll via NATS, ~30s timeout) |
| `talk_reply` | Send a message in the active talk channel |
| `talk_end` | End the conversation |

### iOS Native Client Vision

An iOS biff client connects directly to NATS. Each repo is a workspace. Each active session is a row. Tap a session to `/talk`:

- Multi-repo dashboard showing all active sessions
- Tap any session (human or agent) to open a talk channel
- Messages flow over NATS — instant delivery
- Can steer agents, answer questions, approve decisions
- Can spawn agents (lower priority)

### Feasibility Spike Results (2026-02-25)

All critical unknowns resolved — **`/talk` is feasible:**

1. **MCP tool timeout** — MCP stdio transport tolerates 30s blocking tool calls with no timeout or error.
2. **Async await in MCP** — `asyncio.Event.wait()` inside a tool call holds the call open and returns instantly when set (simulating NATS message arrival).
3. **Early unblock** — Simulated NATS message unblocks listener in <100ms. A 30s listen returns in 1s when a message arrives at 1s.
4. **Agent loop behavior** — Needs live Claude session test. Tool contract is clean: returns message or "no messages", model decides to re-call.

### Dependencies

- NATS relay (shipped in v0.6.0)
- Hook infrastructure (shipped in v0.8.0)
- Notification hook support in Claude Code

### What This Replaces

The old Phase 3 description mentioned "/talk: real-time bidirectional text conversation" without specifying the mechanism. This design uses MCP long-polling over NATS as the transport, making it work from any client (terminal, iOS, another Claude session) without requiring terminal sharing or subprocess handoff.
close_reason: Shipped in v0.9.0 — /talk, /talk_listen, /talk_end with NATS pub/sub, CLI REPL, full test coverage

title: CLI global flags: reference implementation (compliant)
description:
## Status: FULLY COMPLIANT

Biff CLI passes all 5 global flag checks from punt-kit/standards/cli.md.

### What makes it reference-quality

1. **Flag ordering** — `--json`, `--verbose`, `--quiet` in correct order (`src/biff/__main__.py:98-112`)
2. **invoke_without_command=True** — Set on callback with `ctx.invoked_subcommand is None` help block (lines 98, 146-148)
3. **No argv hoisting** — Zero `sys.argv` manipulation
4. **Centralized `_run()` adapter** — All product commands use `_run(commands.X)` with `CommandResult` dataclass. JSON/text branching happens once at lines 160-190. Admin commands (`version`, `serve`, `install`) that don't need relay sessions have acceptable inline checks.
5. **Mutual exclusion** — `if verbose and quiet: raise typer.BadParameter(...)` at lines 115-116

### Pattern: Humble Object Commands

```
CLI  ──▶ _run(commands.who)  ──▶ CommandResult(text, json_data, error)
                                    │
                              _run() handles:
                              - JSON/text branching
                              - stderr for errors
                              - exit codes
                              - --quiet suppression
```

This is the recommended pattern for projects with async commands and relay/session lifecycles. Other projects adopting the standard should reference this implementation.
close_reason: Already compliant. Documented as reference implementation for other projects.

title: Dual-mode MCP/CLI: expose all biff commands as CLI subcommands
description:
## Summary

Every biff operation should be usable from the terminal as `biff <command>`, not just via MCP tools inside Claude Code. This matches the quarry (../ocr) pattern where the same binary is both CLI and MCP server.

## Current State

- `biff serve` — starts MCP server (exists)
- `biff init` — initializes .biff config (exists)
- `biff version` / `biff statusline` — utility commands (exist)
- Communication commands (`write`, `read`, `who`, `finger`, `plan`, `mesg`) — **MCP-only**, no CLI equivalents

## Target State

Add CLI subcommands mirroring every MCP tool:

| CLI Command | MCP Tool | Core Function |
|-------------|----------|---------------|
| `biff write @user "msg"` | `mcp__biff__write` | `relay.deliver()` |
| `biff read` | `mcp__biff__read_messages` | `relay.fetch()` + `relay.mark_read()` |
| `biff who` | `mcp__biff__who` | `relay.get_sessions()` |
| `biff finger @user` | `mcp__biff__finger` | `relay.get_sessions()` filtered |
| `biff plan "msg"` | `mcp__biff__plan` | `relay.update_session()` |
| `biff mesg on/off` | `mcp__biff__mesg` | `relay.update_session()` |

## Architecture

Follow quarry's pattern:

1. **Shared core stays in relay/models/config** — no duplication
2. **MCP tools** (`server/tools/*.py`) remain thin wrappers calling relay
3. **CLI commands** (`__main__.py` or `cli/` submodule) are equally thin wrappers calling the same relay methods
4. **Boundary differences**:
   - CLI: `print()` output, `sys.exit(1)` on error, `asyncio.run()` for async
   - MCP: return strings, errors as strings (session continues)
5. **Formatting shared** — `_format_table()` etc. used by both modes

## Key Decisions

- CLI commands need a relay connection. The `biff serve` command starts a persistent server; CLI commands need ephemeral connections. This means each CLI command will: load config → connect relay → execute → disconnect.
- Output formatting is already server-side (columnar tables with padding). CLI can reuse directly.
- The `update_current_session()` call in MCP tools touches session heartbeat — CLI commands may want a lighter touch (connect, act, disconnect without claiming a session slot).

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `biff write @user "message"` sends a message
- [ ] `biff read` shows unread messages (same columnar format)
- [ ] `biff who` lists active sessions
- [ ] `biff finger @user` shows user status
- [ ] `biff plan "working on X"` sets plan
- [ ] `biff mesg on` / `biff mesg off` toggles availability
- [ ] All commands work without a running MCP server
- [ ] Error handling: connection failures print to stderr, exit 1
- [ ] Tests: unit tests for CLI commands (tier 1-2)
close_reason: Folded into biff-2n1 (Projection Model adoption) as deliverable #2 (CLI duality)

title: Epic: Agentic coordination — multi-agent awareness and worktree safety
description:
## Vision

Enable humans and agents to work simultaneously in the same codebase without stepping on each other. Biff's communication primitives (/who, /write, /wall, /plan) become the coordination layer that prevents filesystem conflicts and gives everyone visibility into what's happening.

## Use Case Foundation

A formal use case analysis (Jacobson-Cockburn v1.1) produced 15 use cases for agentic team coordination. See docs/biff_coordination_use_cases_v0.2.md for the full specification.

### Implementation Tiers

| Tier | Count | Use Cases | Effort |
|------|-------|-----------|--------|
| **T0: Shipped** | 9 | UC1-UC5, UC9, UC10, UC13, UC14 | None — already works |
| **T1: Hooks** | 4 | UC7, UC8, UC11, UC12 | Days — behavioral nudges via existing hook events |
| **T2: Config** | 1 | UC15 (CI/CD notifications) | Days — NATS token + workflow YAML |
| **T3: Dev** | 1 | Mobile surface (cross-cuts UC1, UC2, UC4, UC5, UC9, UC10) | Weeks — separate project |

**Key finding:** 9 of 15 use cases already work today with shipped commands. The gap is not missing commands — it's missing behavioral prompts that nudge agents to use the commands at the right moment.

## The Two Coordination Planes

**Logical plane (cross-machine):** What work is everyone doing?
- /plan shows the bead each agent is working on (auto-expanded with title)
- /who shows all plans across all machines
- Prevents: duplicate work on the same bead (UC11)

**Physical plane (same-machine):** Are we sharing a filesystem?
- /who shows host + directory per session
- Flags same-host+dir overlap (worktree collision risk)
- /write for direct coordination between colliding agents (UC12)
- Agents create git worktrees when conflict detected

## Actors

Four actors share the same identity model (user:tty session):
- **Engineer** — human at terminal (MCP slash commands, CLI)
- **Mobile Engineer** — human away from terminal (iPhone app, biff Python lib in SwiftUI)
- **AI Agent** — Claude Code session (MCP tools via library API)
- **GitHub** — CI workflow or GitHub App with a biff service token

## Component Beads

| Bead | What | Tier | Status |
|------|------|------|--------|
| biff-mx3 | Hook integration (Phase 4: coordination hooks) | T1 | Open — extend existing hooks |
| biff-cs5 | Stop hook: heartbeat + escalation nudge (UC7) | T1 | Open |
| biff-sgl | PreCompact hook: plan survival | T1 | Open |
| biff-60o | Agent team support | T1 | Open — reframed as hook-first |
| biff-6re | GitHub identity + auth (UC15 near-term via service token) | T2 | Open |
| biff-2n1 | Projection Model: library API, CLI duality | — | Closed |
| biff-2lc | Spike: prove TTY + host+dir + targeted write | — | Closed |
| biff-kds | TTY sessions | — | Closed |
| biff-yog | Fix messaging: per-user + TTY mailbox | — | Closed |
| biff-bm0 | /tty command | — | Closed |
| biff-roa | Enriched presence: host+dir in /who | — | Closed |
| biff-klz | /wall: terminal banners with TTL | — | Closed |
| biff-bf8 | /biff y|n — per-user activation | — | Closed |

## Implementation Sequence

Phase 1 (T1 — Immediate): Extend SessionStart hook to inject coordination guidance when multiple sessions detected (UC11, UC12). Extend PostToolUse on PR creation to suggest /write (UC8). Add Stop hook for escalation nudges (UC7). Update AGENT_WORKFLOW.md.

Phase 2 (T2 — Near-term): Provision NATS service token for GitHub Actions. Add biff notification steps to CI workflows (UC15). Document GitHub-as-actor setup.

Phase 3 (T3 — Future project): iPhone app embedding biff Python library in SwiftUI. Separate project like quarry-menubar.

## Success Criteria

Two agents claim different beads in the same repo on the same machine. They detect the conflict via /who, coordinate via /write, create worktrees, and work in isolation. A third agent on a different machine sees everyone's plans via /who and knows what work is claimed. A GitHub Action posts /wall on CI failure and /write to the committer. A human checks /who from their phone and sends a priority redirect to an agent.

title: biff install: deploy git hook templates

title: Speak /wall broadcasts aloud via vox
description:
When a /wall broadcast arrives and voice is enabled, speak the wall message aloud via vox. Wall messages are team-wide announcements — hearing them spoken adds urgency and ensures they aren't missed in a busy session.

## Behavior

- On wall receipt, speak: 'Wall from eric: release freeze — do not push to main.'
- Distinct tone from regular messages — vibe tags like [alert] [serious] or [authoritative]
- Speak once on first receipt. Track last-spoken wall ID to prevent re-speaking on subsequent status bar polls.
- Respect /mesg n (DND) — no audio when messages are off.
- Respect voice_enabled config — no audio when voice is off.
- Use sender's voice if known, otherwise a distinct 'announcement' voice (e.g. 'roger').

## Implementation Path

1. In the shell path that renders wall broadcasts, check voice_enabled
2. Call vox speak with appropriate vibe tags for urgency
3. Track wall ID in a state file to prevent repeat announcements
4. Non-blocking (fire and forget) — don't block the display pipeline
close_reason: Shipped in v0.17.0 — wall broadcasts spoken via vox L1 integration

title: finger should show plan even when user has no active session
description:
finger should show plan even when user has no recent tool calls. In UNIX, finger read the .plan file from disk regardless of login state.

## Root Cause

UserSession bundles ephemeral presence (last_active) with persistent data (plan) in a single storage object with aggressive TTLs:
- NATS KV: 300s TTL — session disappears after 5 minutes
- /who: 120s application TTL — user drops from presence list after 2 minutes

Neither /who nor /finger refresh last_active (read-only), and there is no background heartbeat.

## Revised Design: Long-Lived Sessions + IDLE Display

Design principle: **Sessions persist for 30 days. "Active" is replaced by IDLE time.**

### 1. NATS KV TTL — nats_relay.py:52

_KV_TTL = 300 → _KV_TTL = 2_592_000 (30 days). Sessions survive for 30 days, naturally expiring via NATS KV TTL. No separate plan store needed.

### 2. Rename get_active_sessions → get_sessions — relay.py, nats_relay.py

Remove the ttl parameter. Return all sessions the relay has. NATS KV handles 30-day expiry; LocalRelay adds a 30-day cutoff on read.

### 3. /who shows IDLE column — who.py

Instead of filtering by 120s TTL, show ALL sessions with computed idle time:
  @kai          2m   +  fixing auth
  @eric        3h   +  reviewing PR #42
  @priya       2d   -  on vacation

Sort by user. Idle computed from last_active.

### 4. /finger handles idle state — finger.py

Session always available (within 30 days). Show idle/offline indicator instead of "Never logged in" for known users with stale sessions.

### 5. Shell hook — suppress-output.sh

Update table format to include IDLE column.

### Files Touched

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| src/biff/nats_relay.py | _KV_TTL 300 → 2_592_000, rename method |
| src/biff/relay.py | Rename get_active_sessions → get_sessions, 30-day cutoff in LocalRelay |
| src/biff/server/tools/who.py | Remove TTL filter, add idle computation, new format |
| src/biff/server/tools/finger.py | Handle idle state display |
| plugins/biff/hooks/suppress-output.sh | IDLE column in table |
| tests/ | Update all who/finger tests |

~40-60 lines changed across 6 files.
close_reason: Long-lived sessions (30d TTL) with idle time display. Implemented in fe4ed1c on fix/who-output branch.

title: MCP server dormant mode when biff is not enabled
description:
## Summary

When `.biff.local` is absent or has `enabled = false`, the MCP server should stay dormant:

1. **No NATS connection** — don't connect to relay, don't create streams/consumers
2. **No session registration** — don't appear in /who on other repos
3. **No heartbeat loop** — no background KV writes
4. **No status line** — don't write the PPID-keyed unread file (or write a dormant sentinel)
5. **Tools return guidance** — all tool calls return "biff is not enabled in this repo. Run /biff y to activate."

### Startup check

MCP server startup (`create_server` / lifespan) checks:
1. Find repo root (git rev-parse)
2. Check `.biff` exists — if not, dormant
3. Check `.biff.local` exists and `enabled = true` — if not, dormant
4. Only then proceed with NATS connection and session lifecycle

### PostToolUse hooks

Hooks (PR announce, bead-claim, branch-switch) check the same `.biff.local` state. Silent when dormant.

Depends on biff-dzh (the commands that create/modify `.biff.local`).
close_reason: Shipped in PR #58: dormant mode with DormantRelay, no NATS connection when not enabled

title: Hook: implement Stop hook for unread message check
description:
Z spec audit finding: The Stop hook handler is a stub. No logic checks unread messages or injects reminders.

## Current State
- hook.py cc_stop() (line 613-618) is explicitly a stub: 'full implementation in biff-cs5'
- hooks.json has NO Stop hook registration
- No unread count check occurs at session stop

## Required (from Z spec lines 964-989, FinishResponse operation)
Stop hook that:
1. Checks unread message count (relay query or local unread.json)
2. If unread > 0: inject additionalContext reminder ('You have N unread messages. Consider /read before finishing.')
3. This is a SOFT gate — does not block, only injects context
4. Optionally: check for escalation-pending state (UC7 proposed extension)

## Implementation
1. Add Stop section to hooks/hooks.json
2. Create hooks/stop.sh dispatcher  
3. Implement handle_stop() in hook.py:
   - Read unread count from unread.json or relay
   - If unread > 0: return additionalContext with reminder
   - Exit 0 (never block — soft gate)

## Design Decision  
Should this be a hard gate (exit 2 to block stop) or soft gate (additionalContext injection)?
- Z spec models it as soft (FinishResponse with hdAllow, context injection)
- Hard gate risks infinite loops if agent can't clear messages
- Recommendation: soft gate (match spec)

## Depends on
biff-cs5 (referenced in stub comment) — check if that bead still exists

## Validated by
UC5 (message awareness), UC7 (escalation nudge), UC8 (completion reporting)
close_reason: Implemented Stop hook: hooks.json, stop.sh, handle_stop() reads unread.json, soft gate with /read reminder

title: Status bar scope when messages are TTY-targeted
description:
## Problem (original)

The status bar reads a single unread file per repo. When write/read become TTY-scoped, the status bar needs a strategy for which TTY's messages to show.

## Resolution (2026-02-16)

With the /write design in biff-yog, unread count = user mailbox unread + current TTY mailbox unread. The status bar script needs to:

1. Know the current session's TTY ID (passed via env var or written to a known path by the MCP server)
2. Read from both the user-level unread file and the TTY-level unread file
3. Sum the counts

The MCP server already writes `unread.json` — extend it to include both counts, or write a second file for the TTY inbox.

### Implementation

- `unread.json` becomes: `{"user_count": N, "tty_count": M, "preview": "..."}`
- Status bar displays the sum
- Or: keep two separate files and let the status bar script sum them

## /mesg n suppresses status line unread count

When `/mesg n` is active on a session, the background poller should stop writing unread counts to the PPID-keyed unread file (or write count=0). The status bar stays at `user:tty(0)` even when messages arrive in the mailbox. Messages are NOT dropped — the mailbox still accumulates. Counts reappear when the user runs `/mesg y` or `/read`.

This gives `/mesg n` real meaning beyond a cosmetic flag in `/who` and `/finger`. "Do not disturb" should mean no visual notifications, not just a label.

### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `/mesg n` causes poller to write count=0 (or skip writing) to unread file
- [ ] Status bar shows `user:tty(0)` (plain) while mesg is off
- [ ] Mailbox still accumulates messages — nothing is dropped
- [ ] `/mesg y` resumes normal unread count in status bar
- [ ] `/read` still shows all accumulated messages regardless of mesg state

### Depends on

- biff-yog (per-user mailbox + TTY mailbox /read merge)
- biff-fci (mesg should be per-TTY, not per-user)
close_reason: Implemented: /mesg n writes biff_enabled=false to unread JSON, statusline shows (n) instead of count

title: /biff y|n — per-user biff activation via .biff.local
description:
## Problem

Biff's MCP server connects to NATS in every project where the plugin is installed, consuming streams, consumers, and sessions — even repos where nobody uses biff. This causes resource exhaustion (hit "maximum consumers limit reached" on the demo NATS account).

## Design

Two-layer activation: shared team config + personal toggle.

### Files

| File | Committed | Owner | Contents |
|------|-----------|-------|----------|
| `.biff` | Yes | Team | Team members, relay URL (TOML) |
| `.biff.local` | No (gitignored) | Individual | `enabled = true\|false` (TOML) |

### Commands

| Context | Enable | Disable |
|---------|--------|---------|
| CLI | `biff enable` | `biff disable` |
| MCP slash command | `/biff y` | `/biff n` |

### Behavior

- **`biff enable`** (no `.biff`) → interactive init (team members, relay URL), creates `.biff`, creates `.biff.local` with `enabled = true`
- **`biff enable`** (`.biff` exists, no `.biff.local`) → creates `.biff.local` with `enabled = true`
- **`biff enable`** (already enabled) → no-op
- **`biff disable`** → sets `enabled = false` in `.biff.local`
- **`/biff y`** = `biff enable`
- **`/biff n`** = `biff disable`

### MCP server startup logic

1. Look for `.biff` in repo root — if absent, stay dormant (team hasn't configured biff)
2. Look for `.biff.local` — if absent or `enabled = false`, stay dormant (user hasn't opted in)
3. Both present and enabled → connect to NATS, register session, run heartbeat, activate tools
4. Dormant tools return guidance: "biff is not enabled. Run /biff y or biff enable to activate."

### Default is off

A teammate commits `.biff` to the repo. The repo has biff configured but nobody is active. Each engineer runs `biff enable` or `/biff y` when they want in. No NATS resources consumed until someone opts in.

### What changes

- `biff init` absorbed into first `biff enable` run (no separate init command)
- AGENTS.md markers replaced by `.biff.local` as the activation signal
- No NATS connection, no session, no consumers, no status line when dormant

### Programmatic hooks

PostToolUse hooks (PR announce, bead-claim, branch-switch) only fire when `.biff.local` has `enabled = true`. Silent otherwise.

### Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `biff enable` creates `.biff` (with init flow) on first run, `.biff.local` with `enabled = true`
- [ ] `biff enable` is idempotent
- [ ] `biff disable` sets `enabled = false` in `.biff.local`
- [ ] `/biff y` and `/biff n` MCP commands equivalent to CLI enable/disable
- [ ] MCP server stays dormant (no NATS) when not enabled
- [ ] Status line suppressed when dormant
- [ ] PostToolUse hooks silent when dormant
- [ ] `.biff.local` added to `.gitignore` by `biff enable`
- [ ] Existing `/mesg y|n` unaffected (per-session message reception, separate concept)
close_reason: Core epic shipped across PRs #58 and #60: dormant mode, /biff y|n, .biff.local activation, lazy NATS with POP polling. Remaining dep biff-ka4 (branch-switch hook) is independent enhancement.

title: Wall messages must include sender TTY for multi-session disambiguation
description:
When multiple sessions belong to the same user (e.g. tty2, tty3, 7012aeb2), wall messages show only '@jmf-pobox' with no TTY — making it impossible to tell which session posted the wall.

The status bar shows: '▶ @jmf-pobox: biff v0.12.0 shipped...' but should show '▶ @jmf-pobox (tty3): biff v0.12.0 shipped...' to match the disambiguation /who already provides.

Root cause: wall.py:138 captures from_tty=session.tty_name at post time. If the session wasn't named via /tty before posting, from_tty is empty and stays empty in the stored WallPost.

Fix options:
1. Require or auto-assign TTY name before wall can be posted
2. Fall back to the session_key short ID (e.g. 'f8adaff4') when no TTY name is set
3. Both — auto-assign TTY on session start, always include in wall

Option 2 is the minimum viable fix. Option 3 is better UX.
close_reason: One-line fix: wall.py:138 now uses 'session.tty_name or session.tty' so from_tty is never empty. Named sessions show the name, unnamed sessions fall back to the hex ID. All 825 tests pass.
