title: SessionStart coordination guidance hook (UC11, UC12)
description:
Extend the SessionStart hook to inject coordination guidance when multiple sessions are detected in the same repo.

## Use Case Reference

Implements UC11 (Avoid Duplicate Work) and UC12 (Coordinate File Ownership) from docs/biff_coordination_use_cases_v0.2.md. Implementation tier: T1 (hook only, no new commands).

## Current State

SessionStart collision detection (biff-u8f) already warns when multiple sessions share a worktree. This bead extends that warning into actionable coordination guidance.

## Implementation

Extend handle_session_start() in src/biff/hook.py:

When collision detection finds other sessions in the same repo:
1. List the other sessions (user:tty, plan) in the guidance
2. Inject: "Other sessions are active in this repo. Before starting work, run /who to check what others are working on. Set /plan before beginning to avoid duplicate effort."
3. If sessions share the same worktree (not just repo): additionally suggest /write @other:tty to negotiate file ownership, and recommend git worktrees for isolation.

## Output

additionalContext in the SessionStart hook response, alongside existing collision warnings.

## Testing

Tier 1 unit test: mock handle_session_start with multiple active sessions, verify guidance text appears in output.
close_reason: Shipped: SessionStart collision advisory now includes /who, /plan, /write coordination guidance

title: Phase 4: Hosted relay service (biff teams)
description:
Launch the paid managed biff teams offering on top of the NATS relay.

Since the relay IS a NATS instance, the hosting story is largely
solved. This bead adds the operational and commercial layer:

- Default hosted NATS instance managed by punt-labs
- Team isolation via NATS subject namespacing and per-repo JWTs (biff-3pn)
- Audit logs via JetStream message metadata
- Admin controls: team membership, permissions, onboarding/offboarding
- End-to-end encryption (client-side, keys managed outside relay)
- Compliance exports (message history, audit trails)
- Billing integration ($10/user/month per prfaq)

Self-hosted teams (biff-syr) skip this entirely — they run their own
nats-server via Docker image. This bead is the paid upgrade path.

Exit criteria: a team can sign up for biff teams, get a managed relay
with admin controls, audit logging, and encryption.

title: Adopt org logging standard (punt-kit/standards/logging.md)
description: Biff currently has no persistent log file — only stderr output in verbose mode. To comply with the logging standard: (1) Create logging_config.py with the dictConfig pattern, (2) Add rotating file handler at ~/.punt-biff/logs/biff.log with 0o700 directory permissions, (3) Use standard format string '%(asctime)s [%(levelname)s] %(name)s: %(message)s', (4) Call configure_logging() from CLI and MCP server entry points, (5) PII audit — session keys appear in error messages; assess whether redaction is needed. Reference: punt-kit/standards/logging.md

title: Fix: production plugin.json uses uv run instead of direct binary
description: Production plugin.json uses 'uv run biff serve --transport stdio' to launch the MCP server. This is wrong for marketplace users who install punt-biff via 'uv tool install' — the biff binary is already on their PATH. Using uv run: (1) requires uv installed, (2) can pick up wrong project env if user is in a dir with pyproject.toml, (3) is unnecessary overhead. Fix: match tts plugin pattern — command: biff, args: [serve]. --transport stdio is also redundant since stdio is the default.
close_reason: Fixed in v0.11.3 — plugin.json now uses direct biff binary

title: iPhone companion app for biff (UC1/2/4/5/9/10 mobile surface)
description:
SwiftUI iPhone app embedding the biff Python library for mobile team coordination.

## Use Case Reference

Implements the Mobile Engineer actor across UC1 (who), UC2 (finger), UC4 (write), UC5 (read), UC9 (redirect agent), UC10 (answer agent question) from docs/biff_coordination_use_cases_v0.2.md. Implementation tier: T3 (separate development project).

## What's Needed

1. **Python-to-Swift bridge:** Embed the biff Python library in a SwiftUI app. Options: PythonKit, embedded interpreter, or thin REST gateway wrapping the library.

2. **App UI:** SwiftUI views for /who (team list), /finger (detail), /write (compose), /read (inbox), push notifications.

3. **Session management:** App registers a biff session on launch, deregisters on background/terminate. Push notifications when messages arrive while backgrounded (requires APNs bridge or polling).

4. **Authentication:** App needs NATS credentials and user identity. Could reuse .biff config or have its own provisioning flow.

## Architecture

This is a separate project (like quarry-menubar for quarry). The biff library API is already surface-agnostic — CliContext + commands.* functions work for any consumption surface. The mobile app is a new projection surface, not a biff core change.

## Key Use Cases

- Engineer checks /who from phone while commuting (UC1)
- Engineer redirects agent priority via /write from phone (UC9)
- Engineer answers agent's pending question from phone (UC10)
- Engineer reads incoming messages via push notification (UC5)

## Depends On

Biff library API must be stable. No dependency on process tree, cross-repo discovery, or other V2 features.

title: Status bar update latency regression: wall/talk updates taking 2+ minutes
description:
## Problem

Wall and talk status bar updates are taking 2+ minutes to appear on other sessions. This is a regression — it used to be faster. The sender's own session also experiences delay.

Example: `/wall test` posted on tty1, still not visible on tty2 after 2+ minutes.

## Key observation

**tty2 only updated when a tool was invoked on tty2.** This strongly suggests Claude Code does not continuously poll the statusline command — it only re-runs it around tool invocations. The `_notify_tool_list_changed()` notification may be the only mechanism that triggers a statusline refresh between tool calls, and it may not be working or may not trigger a statusline re-read on other sessions.

This means the problem is likely NOT connection latency or POP timing — the data may be in the file already, but Claude Code's UI isn't reading it until the next tool call.

## Root cause candidates

1. **Claude Code only polls statusline on tool invocations** — if the statusline command only runs when Claude Code has activity (tool calls, user input), idle sessions will never refresh. `_notify_tool_list_changed()` fires on the MCP server that received the message, but that notification only reaches the Claude Code session connected to THAT server — not other sessions.
2. **Connection conservation (POP mode)** — idle sessions nap and only wake every 10s (`_DEFAULT_POP_INTERVAL`). But the key observation above suggests the bottleneck is Claude Code's UI refresh, not NATS.
3. **NATS subscription not established** — if the session's NATS connection dropped and hasn't reconnected, the wall KV watch wouldn't fire. But again, the file may already be written correctly.
4. **Idle threshold transition** — sessions go idle after 120s (`_DEFAULT_IDLE_THRESHOLD`). An idle session disconnects from NATS entirely and relies on POP fetches.

## Testing strategy

**Test wall before talk.** Wall is simpler (KV-based, no talk subscription needed) and exercises the same underlying notification infrastructure. If wall latency is fixed, talk benefits automatically. If wall is broken, talk can't work either.

## Reproduction

1. Open two sessions (tty1, tty2)
2. Post `/wall test` from tty1
3. Observe tty2 status bar — should update within 2-5s, currently takes 2+ minutes
4. Invoke any tool on tty2 — status bar updates immediately

## Investigation needed

- Confirm whether the unread file on tty2 is written promptly (data present but UI not reading)
- Determine Claude Code's statusline polling behavior — is it only on tool invocations?
- Test whether `_notify_tool_list_changed()` triggers a statusline re-read on the same session
- Determine if there is any cross-session notification mechanism available
close_reason: Fixed in v0.10.5 — PR #77. Eliminated POP-mode connection cycling (DES-019).

title: post-commit hook: update plan with commit subject
description:
Git post-commit hook calls `biff hook git post-commit`.

Updates /plan with commit subject line (first line of commit message), prefixed with ✓.
Only overwrites if plan_source is 'auto'.
Gates on .biff + .biff.local enabled. Fail-open (|| true).

Example: after committing 'feat: auto-assign TTY', plan becomes '✓ feat: auto-assign TTY'.

Requires biff-7vp (dispatcher), biff-efk (plan_source).
close_reason: Post-commit hook: ✓ subject plan hint, 3 tests

title: Stop hook: presence heartbeat
description:
## Problem

Add Stop hook that calls biff hook claude-code stop. Two purposes:

1. **Presence heartbeat** — refresh KV entry TTL to prove session is alive to remote observers
2. **Escalation nudge (UC7)** — when the agent finishes a turn with unresolved decisions, inject guidance: "You identified an unresolved decision. Consider escalating via /write @human:tty with the options and trade-offs."

## Use Case Reference

Supports UC7 (Escalate a Decision to a Human) from docs/biff_coordination_use_cases_v0.2.md. The Stop hook is the natural moment to nudge — the agent has finished its turn and is about to hand control back. If it flagged uncertainty during reasoning, the hook can prompt it to escalate before stopping.

## Blocking Question: Are Stop hooks blocking?

If Stop hooks block Claude Code's response rendering, a NATS round-trip (~50-200ms) on every response is unacceptable. If non-blocking, it's free.

## Experiment (2026-03-07)

Installed a user-level Stop hook (~/.claude/settings.json) with a 10-second sleep. Observed ~20s delay before next response appeared. However, this was in a very long session (context near limit, multiple compactions) so the delay may be normal response latency rather than hook blocking.

### Key findings so far

- Stop hooks do NOT fire from plugin hooks.json — only user-level
- The hook definitely fires (log file confirmed 3 runs at 3s each)
- Inconclusive on blocking: 20s delay observed but session was near context limit with high base latency
- Need fresh session test to isolate

## Implementation

1. Add Stop hook to user-level settings (via biff install) since plugin hooks.json doesn't support Stop
2. Handler: biff hook claude-code stop
3. Logic: refresh session heartbeat in relay + check for escalation signals in recent context
4. Return additionalContext with escalation nudge if warranted

title: Spike: validate tools/list_changed in Claude Code
notes: Server-side validation complete (5/5 checks pass). Remaining: Claude Code e2e test — does Claude Code act on notifications/tools/list_changed and show updated tool descriptions? Requires session restart with biff-spike registered in .mcp.json.
close_reason: E2E validated: Claude Code acts on tools/list_changed. Dynamic tool descriptions update in real-time. Full chain confirmed: simulate_message → remove/re-register tool → list_changed notification on SSE → Claude Code refreshes → updated description visible to Claude.

title: Status bar: always emit line 2 to prevent repositioning
description: The status bar currently omits line 2 when no wall is active, causing the bar to jump between 1 and 2 lines. Always emit a blank line 2 so the bar height is stable. This also prepares line 2 for fortune (biff-0ap) — fortune fills the blank, wall overrides both.
close_reason: Stable 2-line status bar with ▶ marker, wall sender display, improved /plan hook guidance

title: NATS namespace collision: repo name is not globally unique
description:
## Bug

NATS KV bucket names and subject prefixes use the bare repo name (e.g. `biff-biff-sessions`), not a globally unique identifier. Two unrelated projects with the same repo name on the shared demo relay will collide — their sessions, messages, and presence data will intermix.

**Additionally**, repo names are not sanitized for NATS-safe characters. GitHub allows dots in repo names (e.g. `socket.io`, `vue.js`), but NATS uses `." as a subject delimiter. A repo named `my.repo` would produce invalid bucket/subject names that break NATS subject hierarchy.

## Root Cause

`NatsRelay` constructs bucket/stream names from `repo_name` (the git directory basename), which is:
1. **Not globally unique** — `punt-labs/biff` and `someone-else/biff` both resolve to `biff`
2. **Not sanitized** — dots, spaces, and other NATS-illegal characters in repo names are passed through verbatim

## Fix

1. Use the full GitHub owner/repo slug (e.g. `punt-labs/biff`) or a hash of the remote URL as the namespace key
2. Sanitize the namespace identifier for NATS: replace dots and illegal characters with dashes or use a hash

Changes needed:
- `BiffConfig.repo_name` — resolve to `owner/repo` from git remote
- `NatsRelay` bucket/stream naming — sanitize for NATS
- `.biff` file format (if repo identity is stored there)
- Existing KV data migration (or accept a clean break)

## Impact

- Two teams using the demo relay with identically-named repos will see each other's sessions and messages (data leak)
- Repos with dots in their name will fail to connect or produce corrupt NATS subjects
close_reason: Fixed in PR #39. NATS namespace now derived from owner/repo slug via git remote origin, with __ delimiter.

title: Epic: biff+vox better together — voice-enabled communication
description: Biff (team communication) and Vox (TTS voice) should integrate deeply so that voice becomes a first-class communication channel. Individual features: voice identity in presence (/who, /finger), talk messages spoken aloud, wall broadcasts spoken aloud, incoming messages read aloud, and voice pairing visible across the system. The voice pairing (e.g. @jmf-pobox + matilda) should be a legible concept throughout biff's presence system.

title: /biff y|n and biff enable|disable: activation commands
description:
## Summary

Implement the four activation commands:

| Context | Enable | Disable |
|---------|--------|---------|
| CLI | `biff enable` | `biff disable` |
| MCP slash command | `/biff y` | `/biff n` |

### `biff enable` / `/biff y`

1. Check for `.biff` in repo root
2. If absent → run interactive init flow (team members, relay URL), create `.biff`
3. Create or update `.biff.local` with `enabled = true`
4. Add `.biff.local` to `.gitignore` if not already present
5. Idempotent — no-op if already enabled

### `biff disable` / `/biff n`

1. Set `enabled = false` in `.biff.local`
2. Idempotent — no-op if already disabled or no `.biff.local`

### Absorbs `biff init`

The existing `biff init` command is replaced by `biff enable`. First run triggers the same interactive flow. Remove `init` as a separate subcommand.

### MCP slash commands

`/biff y` and `/biff n` call the same logic as the CLI commands. These match the BSD `biff y`/`biff n` vocabulary (toggle mail notification).
close_reason: Shipped in PR #58: /biff y|n MCP tool and biff enable/disable CLI commands

title: Plan semantics: plan_source field, overwrite priority
description:
Add plan_source field to session model to track how the plan was set.

Values: 'manual' (user called /plan or bead-claim), 'auto' (git hook, session-start).
Priority: manual > bead > git. Git hooks only overwrite if plan_source is 'auto'.

Provenance prefixes in plan text:
- Manual /plan: no prefix
- Bead claim: no prefix  
- Git post-checkout: → prefix
- Git post-commit: ✓ prefix
- SessionStart branch: → prefix

Stored in UserSession model. plan_source defaults to 'manual' (backwards compatible with existing /plan calls).

This is a data model change — must ship before git hooks (Phase 2) to prevent git hooks from clobbering manual plans.

title: Align command vocabulary with Unix semantics
description:
Current biff commands mismap Unix ancestors. Fix the vocabulary:

## Problem
- `/mesg @user msg` sends messages — but Unix `mesg(1)` only controls reception
- `biff on/off` tool controls reception — but Unix `biff(1)` is mail notification
- `send_message` MCP tool name doesn't match any Unix command

## Correct Mapping
| Slash Command | MCP Tool | Unix Ancestor | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| `/write @user msg` | `write` | `write(1)` | Send a message |
| `/mesg y` / `/mesg n` | `mesg` | `mesg(1)` | Control message reception |
| (product name) | — | `biff(1)` | New mail notification (status line) |
| `/finger` | `finger` | `finger(1)` | Already correct |
| `/who` | `who` | `who(1)` | Already correct |
| `/plan` | `plan` | `.plan` | Already correct |
| `/check` | `check_messages` | `from(1)`/`mail(1)` | Already correct |

## Changes Required
1. Rename MCP tool `send_message` → `write`
2. Rename MCP tool `biff` (on/off) → `mesg`
3. Update slash command `/mesg` → `/write` for sending
4. Update slash command for reception control to `/mesg on` or `/mesg y`
5. Update CLAUDE.md command vocabulary table
6. Update plugin hooks, install.sh, tests
7. Update PR/FAQ if it references command names
close_reason: Tool names aligned: biff→mesg, send_message→write, check_messages→read_messages. All 293 tests pass.

title: mesg on/off should be per-TTY, not per-user
description: Currently /finger shows 'Messages: on' at the user level, but with multi-TTY sessions it should be per-session. A user might want messages off in one session (deep work) and on in another (coordination). The mesg flag lives on UserSession but /finger displays it once for the user header instead of per-TTY line.

title: Adopt CLI standard: complete product surface on CLI
description:
Adopt punt-kit CLI standard (punt-kit-asb). The CLI must expose every capability available via MCP tools and slash commands. The BSD Unix vocabulary IS the product — who, finger, talk, write, wall, read, last, plan, tty, mesg — and it should all work from the terminal.

## New commands needed
- who: list active team members (maps to who MCP tool)
- finger <user>: check what a teammate is working on (maps to finger MCP tool)
- write <user> <message>: send a message (maps to write MCP tool)
- read: check inbox for new messages (maps to read_messages MCP tool)
- wall <message>: broadcast to all teammates (maps to wall MCP tool)
- last: show session login/logout history (maps to last MCP tool)
- plan <text>: set what you're working on (maps to plan MCP tool)
- tty <name>: name the current session (maps to tty MCP tool)
- mesg y/n: control message reception (maps to mesg MCP tool)
- status: current connection state, active sessions, pending messages

## Refactors

- Disable typer rich panels for plain text help
- Hide 'hook' subcommand in --help (internal)
- Add version as both subcommand and implicit (already has version subcommand)

## Existing commands that stay
- talk (already on CLI), version, doctor, install, enable/disable, serve, statusline

title: Install biff MCP server globally in ~/.claude/mcp.json
description: The biff MCP server is currently configured per-project in .mcp.json. Since statusLine is global (~/.claude/settings.json), the MCP server must also be global so biff runs in every session and can update unread counts regardless of which project is open. The install-statusline command should also add the biff server entry to ~/.claude/mcp.json (and uninstall should remove it). Depends on biff-yl1 (per-project unread counts) to avoid cross-project collisions once the server runs globally.
close_reason: Completed in PRs #24-#26 (register MCP server on install/uninstall) and #27 (graceful degradation)

title: Stale unread files: add cleanup on server startup
description:
## Problem

`~/.biff/unread/` accumulates JSON files for every project that ever ran a biff server. No cleanup mechanism exists — servers write files but never delete them. After the slug-based naming change (DES-007a), old `biff.json` files coexist with new `punt-labs__biff.json` files, causing confusion.

## Fix

On server startup (in the lifespan), scan `~/.biff/unread/` and remove files whose repo_name doesn't match any currently-running biff process. Conservative approach: each server writes its own `repo_name` to a lockfile/pidfile in `~/.biff/active/` on startup, removes on shutdown. The status line script (or a periodic task) removes unread files with no matching active file.

Alternative simpler approach: each server removes its own unread file on shutdown (signal handler + lifespan finally block). Stale files from killed processes remain but are harmless (count=0).

## Acceptance

- Server startup cleans up its own stale unread file from prior runs
- Server shutdown writes count=0 to its unread file (already happens via poller)
- Bonus: periodic sweep removes files with count=0 older than 24h
close_reason: PR #40 deletes PPID-keyed unread file in lifespan finally block on shutdown

title: Storage layer: inbox.py and sessions.py
close_reason: MessageStore + SessionStore with 35 tests. JSONL/JSON backed, atomic writes, malformed data recovery.

title: Fix PostToolUse hook matcher to match standalone MCP tool names
description:
PostToolUse hook matcher in hooks/hooks.json uses pattern mcp__plugin_biff(-dev)?_tty__.* which only matches plugin-namespaced tool names. When biff is loaded as a standalone MCP server (not via plugin), tools are named mcp__biff_tty__* or mcp__tty__* — these don't match the hook pattern, so suppress-output and other PostToolUse hooks silently fail.

Fix: Update matcher to mcp__(plugin_biff(-dev)?_)?tty__.* to match both plugin and standalone patterns.

Same class of bug exists in quarry (quarry-fsx) and tts (tts-vyl).
close_reason: Merged PR #118: PostToolUse hook matches standalone MCP tool names

title: Add WebSocket /mcp endpoint with push routing for mcp-proxy
description:
## Why

mcp-proxy is a Go binary that bridges MCP stdio transport to shared daemon processes over WebSocket. Currently, biff only speaks HTTP via `biff serve`, and each Claude Code session spawns a separate biff MCP server process with its own NATS connection. With mcp-proxy, all sessions share one biff daemon with one NATS connection. Push notifications (like `tools/list_changed` when presence changes) get routed to the correct session's WebSocket.

This is the daemon-side work required for mcp-proxy integration. Without this endpoint, mcp-proxy has nothing to connect to.

## What

Add a WebSocket upgrade handler at `/mcp` on the existing `biff serve` HTTP server. The handler should:

- Accept WebSocket upgrade with `session_key` query parameter (e.g., `ws://localhost:8080/mcp?session_key=12345`)
- Read MCP JSON-RPC text messages from the WebSocket
- Route them through the existing MCP tool handler (same code path as stdio)
- Write responses back as WebSocket text messages
- Support multiple concurrent connections (one per Claude Code session), keyed by `session_key`
- Route server-initiated push notifications (e.g., `tools/list_changed`) to the correct session's WebSocket connection
- Clean up session registry entries on WebSocket disconnect

## Technical Notes

- Check what biff's HTTP server framework is (likely starlette or similar) — use its native WebSocket support if available, otherwise `websockets` library
- Session key identifies which Claude Code session the connection belongs to, passed as `?session_key=PID` where PID is the Claude Code process ID resolved by mcp-proxy's process-tree walking
- Over stdio, MCP uses newline-delimited JSON-RPC 2.0. Over WebSocket, each message is a separate WebSocket text frame — no newline delimiter needed
- Biff REQUIRES bidirectional push — this is the harder part of the migration. When NATS delivers a presence change or new message, biff must push `tools/list_changed` to the affected session's WebSocket. This means the NATS subscription callbacks need access to the session registry to find the right WebSocket connection
- Need a registry (dict/map) of `session_key → WebSocket connection` for push routing. Must be thread-safe since NATS callbacks and WebSocket handlers run on different threads/tasks
- The MCP protocol includes notifications (no `id` field) and requests (with `id` field). Both directions must be supported: client→server requests and server→client notifications
- Connection lifecycle: mcp-proxy connects when Claude Code starts a session, disconnects when the session ends (stdin EOF on the proxy side). The daemon must handle abrupt disconnects gracefully (remove from registry, no crash)

## Testing

- mcp-proxy has integration test infrastructure ready (`internal/integration/biff_test.go` placeholder with `integration` build tag) that will exercise this endpoint end-to-end
- Unit tests should cover: session registry add/remove/lookup, message routing, concurrent connection handling
- Integration tests should cover: WebSocket upgrade handshake, full JSON-RPC round-trip, push notification delivery, disconnect cleanup

## Blocked By

Nothing — this can start immediately.

## Blocks

- mcp-proxy slice 5 (biff integration test) — cannot write the integration test until this endpoint exists

title: Phase 2: Network relay and team commands
description:
Enable cross-machine messaging and team-level commands via a hosted NATS relay.

See prfaq.tex for authoritative product vision, phasing, and scope.

## Architecture

punt-labs runs the default hosted relay (NATS + JetStream). Teams connect by having a .biff file in their repo with the relay URL and member usernames. No accounts to create — pip install biff-mcp + clone = connected.

Teams that want data sovereignty can self-host the same single-binary NATS server.

## Data Source & Durability

| Command | Durability | Source | Encryption |
|---------|-----------|--------|------------|
| /plan | Persistent | Relay | Team key (SecretBox) |
| /who | Ephemeral | Relay | None (presence metadata) |
| /finger | Both | Relay | Plan: team key. Online status: none |
| /biff on/off | Ephemeral | Relay | None (session toggle) |
| /mesg | Store-and-forward (POP) | Relay | Recipient's public key (Box) — true E2E |
| /wall | Ephemeral | Relay | Team key (SecretBox) |
| /hive | Ephemeral | Relay | Ephemeral hive key (SecretBox) |
| /send | TBD | TBD | Design pending |
| /cr | TBD | TBD | Design pending |
| /talk | Ephemeral | Relay | Recipient's public key (Box) |
| /pair | Ephemeral | Relay | Recipient's public key (Box) |

## POP Mail Model

The relay follows POP semantics for /mesg: messages are stored on the relay only until the recipient reads them, then discarded. Biff is not a system of record. /wall is ephemeral broadcast — miss it if you're offline.

NATS JetStream consumers with ack-and-delete implement this directly.

## Encryption Design

### Inspiration: ProtonMail

Encryption is invisible to the user. The relay manages the public key directory. Clients encrypt/decrypt locally. The relay routes ciphertext. Like ProtonMail, the server is trusted for key distribution but untrusted for message content.

### Primitives (PyNaCl / libsodium)

- crypto_box (Box): 1:1 messages — Curve25519 + XSalsa20-Poly1305. True E2E.
- crypto_secretbox (SecretBox): Group messages — XSalsa20-Poly1305 with symmetric key.
- PyNaCl v1.6.2, Python 3.13+, actively maintained. Same crypto as Signal, WireGuard, age.

### Identity & Root of Trust

Commit access is the trust boundary:
1. Your GitHub username is your identity
2. You add your username to .biff in the repo (proves commit access)
3. Relay reads .biff from GitHub to verify team membership
4. Auth to relay via GitHub OAuth token

### Keys

- User keypair (Curve25519): per-user, per-repo. Private key in ~/.biff/keys/<repo-hash>.key. Public key in relay's key directory. Generated silently on first connection.
- Team key (symmetric, 32 bytes): per-repo. Generated by relay, distributed to members encrypted with their public keys.
- Hive key (symmetric, 32 bytes): per-hive, ephemeral. Same distribution model. Destroyed when hive dissolves.

### User Experience (transparent)

pip install biff-mcp → clone repo with .biff → first biff serve generates keypair silently → public key registered with relay → team key received encrypted with your public key → user never sees any of this.

### Encryption Per Command

/mesg @kai "text" (true E2E, relay is blind):
  Sender: Box(my_privkey, kai_pubkey).encrypt(message) → relay stores ciphertext
  Kai: Box(kai_privkey, sender_pubkey).decrypt(ciphertext)

/wall "text" (team key, ephemeral broadcast):
  Sender: SecretBox(team_key).encrypt(message) → relay broadcasts ciphertext
  Members: decrypt with cached team key

/plan "text" (team key, persistent on relay):
  Client: SecretBox(team_key).encrypt(plan) → relay stores ciphertext
  /finger: relay returns encrypted plan → requester decrypts with team key

/hive (ephemeral group key):
  Relay generates hive key → distributes via each member's public key
  All hive messages: SecretBox(hive_key).encrypt(message)

### Key Lifecycle

New member: adds username to .biff, commits. First connection → keypair generated, public key registered. Relay encrypts team key with new member's public key → sends it.

Member leaves: removed from .biff. Relay removes public key, rotates team key, re-distributes to remaining members.

Lost key: reconnect → new keypair, re-registered. Unread POP messages lost (acceptable). Team key re-sent with new public key.

### Trust Boundaries

True E2E (relay is blind): /mesg, /talk, /pair — encrypted with recipient's public key. Relay holds ciphertext it cannot decrypt.

Relay-trusted (Proton model): /wall, /plan, /hive — relay generates the symmetric key, so it could theoretically read these. Same trust model as ProtonMail.

Future enhancement: client-side team key generation for zero-knowledge relay. More complex (requires someone online to welcome new members).

Plaintext metadata: from, to, repo, timestamps — relay needs this for routing.

## NATS Implementation

### Why NATS + JetStream

- Purpose-built for messaging. JetStream provides persistence and delivery guarantees.
- KV store for presence (/who, /finger) with TTL, watch API. ~50 LOC.
- JetStream consumers with ack-and-delete implement POP model directly.
- nats-py v2.13.1 (Feb 2026), 145K weekly PyPI downloads, full asyncio.
- Self-hosting: single Go binary (<20MB). nats-server -js.
- Managed hosting: Synadia Cloud (free personal, $49/mo starter).
- Handles 100K msg/s on single node.

### Hosting Model

punt-labs runs the default NATS instance. Free tier with volume limits. Teams needing higher volume or data sovereignty self-host the same binary.

## Open Design Questions

1. Freemium model — volume limits, what's free vs. paid
2. GitHub integration — how relay reads .biff (webhook vs. poll vs. on-connect fetch)
3. /send and /cr — data source, persistence, and encryption model TBD
4. Team key rotation schedule — on membership change only, or periodic?
5. Key pinning / transparency — how to detect relay serving wrong public keys (TOFU vs. key transparency log)

## Client Architecture

New module: biff.relay with:
- RelayClient — wraps nats-py, handles connect/disconnect/send/subscribe
- RelayMessageStore — POP model: pull unread, ack-and-delete
- RelaySessionStore — presence via NATS KV store
- RelayCrypto — wraps PyNaCl, manages keypairs and team key cache

Dual-mode: local (filesystem) or relay, selected by relay_url in .biff file.

## Exit Criteria

A distributed team can communicate end-to-end through biff across machines using the default hosted relay or their own self-hosted instance, with E2E encryption for 1:1 messages and Proton-model encryption for team messages.
close_reason: Phase 2 relay is shipped: cross-machine messaging on Synadia Cloud, NATS JetStream relay, KV presence, wtmp session history, shared streams (DES-016). Remaining encryption work tracked by biff-6re (GitHub identity) and biff-lff (E2E encryption).

title: Status bar message queue: minimum display period, rotation, wall persistence
description:
## Problem

Status bar line 2 has three competing content types (talk, wall, idle) but no temporal management:

1. **Rapid talk messages overwrite** — `_talk_message` is a single string. Three messages in 1s = only the last one visible. Messages 1-2 are lost.
2. **Wall is completely hidden during talk** — priority logic is `talk > wall > idle` with no rotation. A wall announcement (`deploy freeze`) disappears entirely during an active talk session.
3. **No minimum display period** — each message holds line 2 for 0ms before the next one overwrites it.

## Desired behavior

- **Minimum display period** (e.g. 3-5s) — each message holds line 2 before yielding to the next.
- **Message queue** — rapid-fire talk messages queue instead of overwriting. Display in order.
- **Wall persistence** — wall is an announcement that should show through (or rotate with) talk, not be hidden. Wall has higher persistence than talk.
- **Priority + rotation** — when both wall and talk are active, interleave or split display time.

## Current code path

- `_talk_message`: single string in `_descriptions.py`, overwritten on each NATS callback
- `_wall_text`: single string, set by `refresh_wall()`
- `_write_unread_file()`: writes both to JSON, no temporal logic
- `statusline.py:run_statusline()`: reads JSON, applies `talk > wall > idle` priority (line 184-185)
- Claude Code polls the statusline command at its own interval

## Design questions

- Where does the queue live — MCP server (writes different messages to file over time) or statusline script (reads all messages from file, picks which to show)?
- How does minimum display period interact with Claude Code's polling interval?
- Should queued messages that are never displayed (because newer ones arrived) be dropped or shown briefly?
close_reason: PR #79 merged — display queue for status bar rotation

title: NATS namespace scoping: isolate relay resources per repo
description:
## Problem

NATS KV bucket (biff-sessions) and JetStream stream (BIFF_INBOX) use hardcoded names. Any NATS server serving multiple repos has complete data collision: sessions, messages, and future features (wall, tty) all bleed across repo boundaries.

This is the normal deployment scenario — a company self-hosts one NATS server and has multiple repos with .biff files pointing at it. Every engineer's /who shows sessions from every repo, messages cross-deliver, plans leak. The demo relay makes it worse (strangers collide) but it's broken for any multi-repo setup.

Local relay is already repo-scoped by directory path (/tmp/biff/{repo_name}/). NATS is not.

## Root cause

NatsRelay receives (url, auth, client_name) but zero knowledge of which repo it serves. The repo identity is available in load_config() but never flows to the relay.

```
load_config()
  -> repo_root = find_git_root()              # knows the repo
  -> compute_data_dir(repo_root, prefix)       # /tmp/biff/{repo_name}/ <- scoped

create_state(config, data_dir)
  -> LocalRelay(data_dir=data_dir)             # inherits repo scope from path
  -> NatsRelay(url=..., name=f"biff-{user}")   # NO repo info passed
```

## Design

### 1. Flow repo identity to the relay

Add repo_name: str = "_default" to BiffConfig. Populate from sanitize_repo_name(repo_root.name) in load_config(). "_default" is the fallback when not in a git repo (matches local relay convention).

### 2. Sanitize repo names for NATS

NATS constraints: bucket names allow alphanumeric/dash/underscore only. Subject dots are level separators. Wildcards (* >) are reserved.

```python
def sanitize_repo_name(name: str) -> str:
    clean = name.replace(".", "-").replace(" ", "-")
    return "".join(c for c in clean if c.isalnum() or c in "-_") or "_default"
```

### 3. Namespace all NATS resources

NatsRelay.__init__ accepts repo_name, uses it everywhere:

- KV bucket: biff-{repo}-sessions (was: biff-sessions)
- Stream: BIFF_{repo}_INBOX (was: BIFF_INBOX)
- Subject prefix: biff.{repo}.inbox (was: biff.inbox)
- Client name: biff-{repo}-{user} (was: biff-{user})

The subject hierarchy biff.{repo}.inbox.{user} also opens the door for future subjects:

- biff.{repo}.wall (wall broadcasts)
- biff.{repo}.talk.{session} (talk streams)

### 4. Migration

No migration. Sessions rebuild on next heartbeat. Unread messages in old stream are lost — acceptable pre-1.0. Old resources (biff-sessions bucket, BIFF_INBOX stream) are orphaned and will be purged by NATS TTL or can be manually deleted.

### 5. Test isolation

Tests that hit a real NATS server (tiers 3b, 3c) must use a repo name that cannot collide with other test runs or real usage. Each test session generates a unique repo name:

- Format: _test-{uuid4_short} (e.g., _test-a1b2c3d4)
- Leading underscore ensures it cannot collide with real repo names
- UUID suffix ensures parallel test runs don't collide with each other
- Test fixtures create NatsRelay with this generated repo_name
- Test teardown deletes the KV bucket and stream (already done, but now with the scoped names)

NOTE: The existing CI failures (test_biff_off_visible_to_other, test_both_visible_in_who) show plan text leaking between tests — sessions from one test bleed into the next. This is the same class of isolation bug. Subprocess test fixtures must also ensure each test gets a clean relay with no leftover state.

For local relay tests: already isolated via tmp_path, no change needed.

### 6. Edge cases

| Case | Behavior |
|------|----------|
| Not in a git repo | repo_name = "_default" |
| Two repos same name, different paths | Same collision local relay has. Not a regression. |
| Repo renamed | New NATS resources, old orphaned. Sessions rebuild. |

## Files changed

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| src/biff/models.py | Add repo_name: str = "_default" to BiffConfig |
| src/biff/config.py | Populate repo_name from repo_root.name, add sanitize_repo_name() |
| src/biff/nats_relay.py | Accept repo_name in __init__, use in bucket/stream/subject names |
| src/biff/server/state.py | Pass config.repo_name to NatsRelay() |
| tests/conftest.py or test fixtures | Generate unique _test-{uuid} repo name per test session |
| tests/test_nats_e2e/ | Pass repo_name through fixtures |
| tests/test_hosted_nats/ | Same |
| tests/test_subprocess/ | Investigate and fix session leakage between tests |
| tests/test_config.py | Test sanitize_repo_name(), test repo_name in BiffConfig |

## Verification

1. Quality gates: ruff, pyright, pytest (tiers 1-2)
2. Subprocess tests (tier 3): CI failures resolved, no cross-test leakage
3. NATS E2E (tier 3b): two test users in same repo see each other, different repos isolated
4. Hosted NATS (tier 3c): same isolation test against real Synadia Cloud
5. Manual: two repos on same NATS server, /who in one does not show sessions from the other
close_reason: Merged PR #33: all NATS resources scoped by repo_name

title: Dev plugin install not reflecting local version
description:
## Problem

Local plugin installs (via --plugin-dir or symlink) break the status
line. Claude Code spawns local plugin MCP servers under a separate
claude process tree. The ancestor walk in find_session_key() (DES-011a)
finds different claude PIDs for the MCP server vs the status line:

- MCP server walks up → finds claude PID 27528
- Status line walks up → finds claude PID 30234
- MCP writes to 27528.json, status line reads 30234.json → not found

Status bar shows bare "biff" instead of "kai:tty1(3)".

## Impact

Dev-only. Marketplace installs work correctly (both processes share
the same claude ancestor). Production users are unaffected.

## Workaround

Use marketplace installs for status line testing. Local plugin
development can ignore the status bar.

## Potential fixes (from DES-011b)

- MCP server writes a PID mapping file on startup
- Status line scans all unread files, picks freshest for this repo
- Use NATS session key instead of PID for file naming

None implemented. Low priority — cosmetic dev-tooling issue.

description:
Implement NatsRelay class satisfying the existing Relay Protocol. NATS KV for sessions/presence, JetStream consumer with ack-and-delete for POP message semantics. Add nats-py dependency. Unit tests against local NATS server.

Part of Phase 2 milestone 1: local NATS on single machine.

title: Status bar notification: write unread state for Claude Code status line
description:
Dynamic tool descriptions are model-visible only — the human user has no visual notification in Claude Code UI. The spike confirmed this UX gap.

Claude Code's status bar is a configurable shell command (runs locally, can read files). Pattern: biff writes unread state to a well-known file (~/.biff/data/unread.json), and a status bar script reads it to display a live unread count.

Deliverables:
1. Biff writes unread summary to a local file whenever unread count changes
2. Provide a sample status bar script users can configure in Claude Code settings
3. Document setup in README

Depends on the message watcher (biff-9xj) which detects unread count changes.

title: Voice mode: auto-speak incoming messages via vox
description:
Biff-native voice mode. When enabled, biff synthesizes incoming messages as audio using punt-tts. Biff owns the audio experience — it decides what to vocalize, picks voices, and controls pacing. punt-tts is the rendering engine.

## Projection Model (ref: choosing-the-right-projection)

Biff has two caller contexts that need voice. The right projection differs per caller:

1. Shell hooks (status-line.sh, wall display) → CLI projection (`tts speak --text '...' --ephemeral`). These are already shell scripts. CLI is the natural same-machine, different-process projection.

2. Python MCP server (background poller, talk_listen) → Library projection when punt-tts is bundled (`from punt_tts.core import TTSClient`). Same process, no serialization overhead. This is the MCP-to-Library pattern from LangLearn — the orchestrator imports the library, not the MCP server.

Both projections coexist. Shell hooks use CLI. Python server code uses library import when available, CLI fallback when not.

## Packaging

`pip install punt-biff[voice]` adds punt-tts as optional dependency. When installed, Python code imports TTSClient directly (library projection). When not installed, voice features are silently disabled — text-only behavior unchanged. Shell hooks check `which tts` independently.

## Config

Toggle: voice_enabled in .tts/config.md (shared with punt-tts plugin). One switch controls voice for the whole session. No biff-specific toggle.

Per-sender voices in .biff config:
```toml
[voice.senders]
kai = "roger"
priya = "aria"
eric = "charlie"
```

## UX

- /read — After displaying inbox table, speaks each unread message: 'kai says: hey, ready for review?' Per-sender voices. Skips if no unread.
- /mesg n (DND) — Voice suppressed. DND means DND.

## Graceful Degradation

- punt-tts not installed → ImportError caught, voice silently disabled
- tts CLI not on PATH → shell hooks skip voice calls
- No ElevenLabs API key → TTSClient.synthesize raises, caught and logged

title: post-checkout hook: update plan from branch
description:
Git post-checkout hook calls `biff hook git post-checkout "$1" "$2" "$3"`.

Fires on git checkout, git switch, and git worktree add. Receives: previous HEAD, new HEAD, branch flag (1=branch checkout, 0=file checkout).

When branch flag is 1:
1. Read new branch name
2. If branch contains a bead ID pattern (e.g., biff-ka4), resolve title via `bd show --format=oneline`
3. Call biff plan with → prefix. Set plan_source=auto.
4. If switching to main/master, clear the plan.

Why git hook instead of PostToolUse Bash regex (original design):
- Catches git switch, git checkout, and worktree operations
- Fires on manual git operations outside Claude Code
- No fragile regex matching of bash commands
- Reliable: git post-checkout is a well-defined hook

Gates on .biff + .biff.local enabled. Fail-open (|| true).
Requires biff-7vp (dispatcher), biff-efk (plan_source).
close_reason: Post-checkout hook: plan hint file + PostToolUse pickup, 11 tests

title: TTY sessions: make MCP server instances first-class
description:
## Problem

Biff collapses identity to the GitHub login — one user = one session. But a single user can have multiple Claude Code windows, multiple agents, or multiple worktrees running simultaneously. Today these stomp each other's session state (plan, mesg, presence).

Unix solved this with ttys: `who` showed each terminal separately, `write user pts/1` targeted a specific one, `mesg n` was per-tty.

## Design

Make sessions first-class with a (user, tty) identity instead of just user.

### TTY identity

Each MCP server instance auto-generates a unique tty ID at startup (short random ID or PID). The user or agent can then name it with `/tty <name>`:

```
> /tty migration
TTY: migration
```

Like Claude Code's `/rename` — no flags, no config, just name it when you want. Unnamed sessions keep the auto-generated ID.

### Wire format

`UserSession` gains a `tty: str` field. Session keys in the relay become `(user, tty)` instead of just `user`. Default tty is auto-generated for backwards compatibility (single-session users see no change).

### Command changes

- `/tty <name>` — name the current session (like `/rename` in Claude Code)
- `/who` shows each session separately
- `/write @jim:migration "schema approved"` targets a specific session
- `/write @jim "heads up"` delivers to all of jim's sessions (or default)
- `/finger @jim:migration` shows that specific session
- `/plan` and `/mesg` are per-session (already are, just keyed by tty now)
- `/wall` hits all sessions for all users

### Status bar

Each session's status bar reflects its own unread count, independent of other sessions for the same user.

## Motivation

- Human + multiple agents: each agent is a named session, human can target or broadcast
- Multiple Claude Code windows: no longer stomp each other
- Matches Unix semantics exactly — `who`, `write`, `mesg` all worked per-tty
close_reason: Core TTY session lifecycle landed in PR #35. Remaining items split to biff-bm0 (/tty command) and biff-ax7 (@user:tty addressing).

title: /wall: team broadcast with duration-based expiry on status line
description:
## Problem

Teams need a broadcast channel for time-sensitive announcements: release freezes, deploy windows, coordination directives. Regular messages go to inboxes and require recipients to `/read`. There's no way to push an announcement that's immediately visible to everyone without action on their part.

## Design (updated 2026-02-16)

### Command semantics: /wall = terminal banners with TTL

`/wall` addresses **terminals** (not users). Immediate visibility on status bar + tool descriptions. TTL-based expiry. Complements `/write` which addresses **user mailboxes** (see biff-yog).

### Addressing

| Command | Target |
|---------|--------|
| `/wall "msg"` | All terminals (team broadcast) |
| `/wall @tty1 "msg"` | Specific terminal (requires named TTY via biff-bm0) |
| `/wall @tty1:tty2 "msg"` | Multiple terminals |
| `/wall clear` | Clears active banner |

`@` is the universal addressing sigil, consistent with `/write`.

### Duration

```
/wall "release freeze — do not push to main"          → default duration (TBD)
/wall "deploy window open" 2h                          → 2 hour expiry
/wall "sprint ends friday" 5d                          → long-lived
```

Duration format: `30m`, `2h`, `1d`, `5d`.

### Display

Status bar: `biff(3) WALL @kai: release freeze — do not push to main`

Visible to Claude via tool description changes (same push notification mechanism as unread counts). No `/read` required.

### Behavior

- One active wall per team (broadcast) or per-terminal (targeted). New replaces old.
- Expires automatically at `created_at + duration`.
- `/wall clear` removes early.
- Wall does NOT go into `/read` inboxes — it is a banner, not a message.

### When to use /wall vs /write

| Scenario | Command | Why |
|----------|---------|-----|
| Tell eric to review a PR | `/write @eric` | Durable, eric reads when available |
| Tell agent2 to pause | `/write @eric:agent2` | Durable, agent2 reads on next `/read` |
| Announce release freeze | `/wall "freeze" 4h` | Immediate visibility on all terminals |
| Direct an agent urgently | `/wall @agent2 "stop" 5m` | Immediate banner (requires named TTY) |
| Broadcast to all terminals | `/wall "pull latest"` | All terminals see it now |

`/write` = "read this when you can" (inbox, POP).
`/wall` = "see this right now" (banner, TTL).

### Wire format

```
message: str, sender: str, created_at: datetime, expires_at: datetime
```

NATS: KV key `wall.{repo}` (broadcast) or `wall.{repo}.{tty}` (targeted).
Local: field in shared state file.

### What needs to be built

1. Wall relay storage — KV entry per team/terminal with TTL.
2. Wall status bar integration — status line script checks for active wall.
3. Wall tool description integration — same mechanism as unread count notifications.
4. `/wall` tool registration and command prompt.
5. `/wall clear` subcommand.

### Dependencies

- **biff-bm0** (`/tty <name>`) — required for targeted `/wall @tty` to be ergonomic. Without it, targeting is `/wall @a1b2c3d4`.

### Unix lineage

BSD `wall(1)` — write all — wrote to every logged-in terminal. Biff adds duration-based persistence (status lines persist unlike terminal scroll) and explicit clearing.
close_reason: Merged in PR #51. Wall feature with duration-based expiry, poll-based cross-session refresh, status bar integration. DES-011a session key fix. Dev/prod namespace isolation with biff-dev plugin name, regex hook matchers, release/restore scripts.

title: No session logout on Claude Code exit
description: When a Claude Code session ends (MCP server shuts down), the session persists in storage indefinitely. app.py lifespan finally block closes the relay connection but does NOT remove the session. The lifespan context manager has access to ServerState (which has user, tty, relay) — the cleanup point is clear. Design question: should this also be a SessionEnd hook that calls a logout tool, or is server lifespan cleanup sufficient?

title: MCP server NATS relay does not auto-reconnect after connection drop
description:
## Root Cause

`_ensure_connected()` (nats_relay.py:154) short-circuits when `self._js` and `self._kv` are cached — it never checks `self._nc.is_closed`. When the NATS TCP connection drops mid-session:

1. Connection established, `_js`, `_kv`, `_nc` all set
2. NATS drops (network blip, idle timeout, SSL error), `_nc.is_closed` becomes `True`
3. Tool call arrives, calls `_ensure_connected()`
4. `_js is not None and _kv is not None` → returns stale cached handles (line 154)
5. `kv.put()` tries to use the dead connection → `ConnectionClosedError`
6. Error propagates uncaught through FastMCP → user sees 'nats: connection closed'

The `nc is None or nc.is_closed` check (line 159) that would create a new connection is never reached.

## Evidence (2026-03-11)

- 9 `biff mcp` processes running, 8 have zero network file descriptors (connections gone, never re-established)
- Only the newest process (PID 33161, started 5:15PM) has a live NATS connection
- `biff doctor` confirms relay is reachable — the server-side is fine
- Python 3.14 SSL error (`APPLICATION_DATA_AFTER_CLOSE_NOTIFY`) on connection teardown may be triggering the initial drop

## Fix

Add connection liveness check to the `_ensure_connected()` fast path:

```python
if self._js is not None and self._kv is not None:
    if self._nc is not None and not self._nc.is_closed:
        return self._js, self._kv
    # Connection died — clear cached handles and reconnect
    self._js = None
    self._kv = None
```

## Additional hardening

- Catch `ConnectionClosedError` in tool handlers, clear cached state, retry once
- Add `disconnected_cb` that clears `_js` and `_kv` so the next call reconnects
- Log connection drops with structured data for diagnosis

title: Epic: Hook integration architecture (DES-017)
description:
## Vision

Make biff a continuous presence in the developer workflow via systematic hook integration. Biff commands (/who, /write, /plan, /wall) are the vocabulary. Hooks are the nervous system that keeps the vocabulary current.

## Architecture (DES-017)

Two hook layers, both calling biff hook <layer> <event> — thin shell dispatchers, all logic in versioned Python:

1. **Claude Code hooks** (plugin hooks/hooks.json) — agent lifecycle: SessionStart, SessionEnd, Stop, PreCompact
2. **Git hooks** (installed by biff install) — code lifecycle: post-checkout, post-commit, pre-push

## Phases

### Phase 1: Session Lifecycle ✅
- biff hook CLI command group (dispatcher foundation)
- SessionStart: auto /tty, set /plan from branch, check unread
- SessionEnd: immediate session cleanup (eliminate ghost sessions)
- Plan auto-expand: bead ID → title
- Plan semantics: append vs. overwrite with plan_source field

### Phase 2: Git Hooks ✅
- post-checkout → update /plan with branch name
- post-commit → update /plan with commit subject
- pre-push → suggest /wall for default branch pushes
- biff install deploys git hook templates

### Phase 3: Heartbeat & Context
- Stop: presence heartbeat (biff-cs5)
- PreCompact: snapshot plan to survive compaction (biff-sgl)
- SessionStart resume/compact: refresh presence, re-announce plan

### Phase 4: Coordination Hooks (NEW — from use case analysis)

Supports UC7, UC8, UC11, UC12 from docs/biff_coordination_use_cases_v0.2.md. These are T1 (hook-only) implementations that enable agentic coordination without new biff commands.

- **SessionStart (extend):** When collision detection finds multiple sessions in same repo, inject coordination guidance — "Run /who before claiming work. Set /plan to avoid duplicate effort." (UC11, UC12)
- **PostToolUse on PR creation (extend):** Already suggests /wall. Add /write @human suggestion with PR link. (UC8)
- **Stop (extend biff-cs5):** Escalation nudge — when agent finishes turn with unresolved decisions, suggest /write @human:tty. (UC7)
- **TeammateIdle:** Natural moment for agent to check /read for redirections from human. (UC9)

## Key Design Decisions
- Thin dispatcher pattern (learned from Entire.io): no logic in shell, all in Python
- Plan overwrite with provenance prefix and plan_source priority (manual > bead > git)
- Fail-open everywhere (git hooks: || true; Claude Code hooks: exit 0 on error)
- Gate all hooks on .biff + .biff.local enabled

title: Add --json global flag to CLI
description:
Add --json global flag for machine-readable output, per punt-kit CLI standard (cli.md §Machine-Readable Output).

## Use case 1: Claude agent teams

Claude Code agent teams (https://claudefa.st/blog/guide/agents/agent-teams) are independent Claude sessions that coordinate via shared task lists and a mailbox. Teammates run CLI commands in their own shells — they do not have MCP access to tools from other sessions.

Today a teammate that wants to check team presence runs `biff who` and gets columnar text:

```
▶  USER     TTY    IDLE  PLAN
   @kai     tty1   2m    reviewing PR #50
   @eric    tty2   15m   fixing relay tests
```

The agent must parse human-formatted output to extract structured data. With `--json`:

```json
[
  {"user": "kai", "tty": "tty1", "idle_seconds": 120, "plan": "reviewing PR #50"},
  {"user": "eric", "tty": "tty2", "idle_seconds": 900, "plan": "fixing relay tests"}
]
```

This makes biff a first-class coordination tool for agent teams — teammates can programmatically check presence, read messages, and query status without MCP.

## Use case 2: Scripts, CI, and tooling

Standard agentic integration: scripts, CI pipelines, and other tools parse JSON output. `biff who --json | jq` works. Human-readable formatting goes to stderr or is suppressed.

## Design

- `--json` is a **global flag** on the typer app, not per-subcommand
- When set, all stdout is valid JSON; human-readable messages go to stderr
- Errors: `{"error": "<message>"}`
- List commands return arrays; detail commands return objects
- Pattern: `bd --json list`, `bd --json show <id>` (beads reference implementation)

## Scope

Every existing and future CLI subcommand that produces output. Priority order:
1. `who`, `finger`, `read` — agent team coordination
2. `doctor`, `version` — scripting basics
3. Future CLI subcommands (biff-9n9) get --json for free

## Standard

Adopts punt-kit CLI standard (cli.md §Machine-Readable Output).
close_reason: Folded into biff-2n1 (Projection Model adoption) as deliverable #3 (--json global flag)

title: biff install and biff doctor commands
description:
## Problem

There is no end-user install process for biff. Users have to manually hack plugin symlinks and marketplace entries. The README says `pip install biff-mcp` + `biff install-statusline` but that only installs the MCP server — not the plugin (slash commands like /who, /write, /mesg).

## Reference

See quarry-mcp (../ocr) for the pattern:
- `quarry install` — creates data dirs, downloads model, configures MCP via `claude mcp add`
- `quarry doctor` — read-only environment diagnostics
- `install.sh` — curl one-liner bootstrap script
- Implementation: `src/quarry/doctor.py` (CheckResult dataclass, run_install, check_environment)

## Scope

### biff install
- Register MCP server in Claude Code (`claude mcp add` or direct JSON config)
- Install the biff plugin into Claude Code's plugin directory
- Set up status line (absorb current `biff install-statusline` functionality)
- Idempotent — safe to run multiple times

### biff doctor
- Check gh auth is working (identity resolution)
- Check MCP server is configured
- Check plugin is installed and commands are registered
- Check status line is configured
- Check .biff file exists in current repo (optional/warning)
- Report version info

### biff uninstall
- Reverse of install: remove MCP server, plugin, restore status line
- Absorb current `biff uninstall-statusline`

### Cleanup
- Deprecate/remove `biff install-statusline` and `biff uninstall-statusline` (absorbed into install/uninstall)
- Update README Quick Start to use `biff install`
- Update CHANGELOG

title: Create manual biff-dev plugin test script
MCP write tool should fire-and-forget relay.deliver()
description:
The `write` MCP tool blocks on `await state.relay.deliver()` but is a side-effect — the caller doesn't need to wait for message delivery to complete. Per punt-kit/standards/cli.md §Sync vs Async, side-effect MCP tools on long-lived servers should fire-and-forget.

Current behavior: tool awaits delivery, then returns confirmation string.
Expected behavior: fire delivery as background task, return confirmation immediately.

Since biff is async-native (NATS), the fix is `asyncio.create_task(state.relay.deliver(...))` instead of `await state.relay.deliver(...)`. The confirmation string ("Message sent to {display}") is already accurate regardless of delivery completion.

Low priority — delivery is fast over NATS, so the blocking is negligible in practice. But it sets the wrong precedent.

Reference: punt-kit/standards/cli.md §Sync vs Async
close_reason: MCP write tool now fire-and-forget via asyncio.create_task with error logging callback

title: Column-constrained table formatter: 80-char wrapping for /who, /last, /read
description:
Extract shared table formatter from duplicate /who and /last _format_table() code. Target 80-char total width. Fixed columns (NAME, TTY, IDLE, S, HOST) get static widths. DIR truncated to last path component. PLAN and other variable columns wrap within their column boundary with continuation lines indented to column start. Applies to /who, /last, /read. /finger uses free-form layout (separate concern).

## Architecture

One public function format_table(), one ColumnSpec dataclass in _formatting.py. Purely functional.

### ColumnSpec
- header: str, min_width: int, fixed: bool = False, align: Literal['left', 'right'] = 'left'
- fixed=True columns grow to fit content but stop there
- fixed=False is the variable column (exactly one per table), gets remaining width

### Constants
- _TABLE_WIDTH = 80 (product decision, not terminal detection)
- _COL_SEP = '  ' (two spaces)
- Prefix: 3 chars ('▶  ' for header, '   ' for data rows)

### Helpers
- _visible_width(s) — strips ANSI for width measurement, never strips output
- _last_component(path) — DIR truncation: /Users/kai/biff → biff

### Wrapping Algorithm
1. Compute fixed col widths: max(min_width, max content width)
2. Variable budget = 80 - prefix(3) - sum(fixed) - separators
3. textwrap.wrap(var_text, budget) with or [''] guard
4. Continuation lines: ' ' * var_offset + chunk

### Column Budgets (/who)
NAME(4) + TTY(3) + IDLE(4) + S(1) + HOST(4) + DIR(3) + seps + prefix ≈ 32 → PLAN gets ~48 chars

## Migration

| Tool | File | Current | After |
|------|------|---------|-------|
| /who | who.py | 30-line _format_table() | _WHO_SPECS + format_table() |
| /last | last.py | 42-line _format_table() | _LAST_SPECS + format_table() |
| /read | messaging.py | 6-line inline | _READ_SPECS + format_table() |

_sanitize_plan() stays in who.py (semantic, not formatting).

## Build Sequence
1. Core formatter + helpers in _formatting.py (~90 lines)
2. Tests in test_formatting.py
3. Migrate /who
4. Migrate /last
5. Migrate /read
6. Full quality gates + DESIGN.md entry (DES-014)
close_reason: Shipped in PR #57. Shared 80-column table formatter for /who, /last, /read with variable-column wrapping.

title: REPL: talk command — modal conversation mode
description:
## Problem

talk is not available in the REPL dispatcher. Typing talk @user returns
"Unknown command." The standalone biff talk creates its own session, but
the REPL already has a session with NATS subscriptions.

## Design

talk @user in the REPL enters a modal conversation mode (like BSD talk):

1. Enter talk mode — print "Connected to @kai. Type end or Ctrl-C to exit."
2. Lines typed go to @kai via relay.deliver()
3. Incoming messages print inline via existing NATS subscription
4. "end" or Ctrl-C exits back to the REPL prompt

The REPL's stdin thread + asyncio bridge stays active. Talk mode just
changes the dispatch target: lines go to deliver() instead of dispatch().
The notification subscription already handles incoming messages.

Optional opening message: talk @kai ready for review?

title: Talk v2: status-line auto-read with mutual agreement
description:
Redesign /talk to use status-line display instead of talk_listen blocking.

CX model:
- /write + /read = async mailbox (sender writes, recipient must explicitly /read)
- /talk = mutual presence (both agree, then auto-read each other on status bar)

When both parties have an active /talk session with each other, incoming messages auto-display on the status line (0-2s, no LLM). Outbound is /talk message (one LLM round-trip, same as /write). No talk_listen blocking needed for the human path.

Key pieces:
1. /talk @user registers intent (MCP tool, sets talk partner)
2. Status line detects active talk session and renders message body (not just count)
3. Message queue with minimum display duration (N seconds per message)
4. /talk end clears the session
5. talk_listen remains for agent-to-agent use cases

Constraints from DESIGN.md:
- Status line polls every 0-2s (DES-004/DES-011)
- 80-char hard limit (DES-014)
- Unread JSON file is the shared state between MCP server and status line (DES-011)

title: Hosted NATS E2E tests hang: session-scoped async fixtures deadlock
description:
The hosted NATS E2E tests (tier 3c, tests/test_hosted_nats/) hang on every run. Zero tests complete. The test suite connects to NATS successfully (JetStream infrastructure appears on the Synadia dashboard) but the first test body never finishes.

Root cause (narrowed, not yet proven): The hosted conftest uses session-scoped async fixtures (kai_relay, eric_relay) to conserve NATS connections (Synadia starter = 5 max). pytest-asyncio with asyncio_default_test_loop_scope=function creates a per-test event loop, but session-scoped async fixtures need a session-scoped loop. The test module sets loop_scope='session' via pytestmark, but this conflicts with the pyproject.toml default.

The local NATS E2E tests (tier 3b) work fine because they use function-scoped fixtures — a fresh NatsRelay per test.

Evidence:
- Raw nats.connect() works (proved with standalone script)
- JetStream resources appear on Synadia dashboard during test run
- Tests collect (9 selected) but hang on first test_plan_visible_via_who
- Local NATS tests: 42/42 pass with function-scoped fixtures
- Changing asyncio_default_test_loop_scope to session did not fix the hang

Impact: We have zero functional integration tests against the hosted relay. All relay testing is against localhost nats-server. This means hosted-specific issues (TLS, auth, latency, connection limits) are untested.

Options to investigate:
1. Convert hosted fixtures to function-scoped (accept more connections per run)
2. Debug the exact await that hangs (add timeout/logging to _ensure_connected)
3. Move to pytest-asyncio 1.x session loop handling
4. Replace session-scoped NatsRelay with a session-scoped raw nats.Client that gets shared
close_reason: Fixed: loop_scope alignment + three consumer leak sources eliminated

title: /last command
description:
## Design: /last — session history (wtmp)

Faithful to Unix `last(1)`, which reads the wtmp file to show login/logout history. This is about **sessions**, not messages.

### Unix Model Mapping

| Unix File | What It Stores | Biff Equivalent | Command |
|-----------|---------------|-----------------|---------|
| utmp (`/var/run/utmp`) | Who is logged in NOW | NATS KV `biff-{repo}-sessions` | `/who` |
| wtmp (`/var/log/wtmp`) | Historical login/logout log | **New**: JetStream stream `biff-{repo}-wtmp` | **`/last`** |
| lastlog (`/var/log/lastlog`) | Last login per user | Derivable from wtmp stream | `/finger` |

### NATS Primitives

Two primitives, one per file:

- **KV bucket** (existing) — `biff-{repo}-sessions` — stores `UserSession` records with 3-day TTL (liveness grace period for laptop-closed scenarios). This is utmp. Powers `/who`.
- **JetStream stream** (new) — `biff-{repo}-wtmp` — append-only event log with `LIMITS` retention and **30-day `max_age`**. This is wtmp. Powers `/last`.

### Event Model

```python
class SessionEvent(BaseModel):
    model_config = ConfigDict(frozen=True)

    user: str               # "kai"
    tty: str                # hex session ID
    tty_name: str           # "tty1"
    host: str               # "kais-mbp"
    directory: str          # working directory at time of event
    plan: str               # plan at time of event
    event: Literal["login", "logout"]
    timestamp: datetime     # login: session start time; logout: last_active time
```

### Event Sources

- **login**: Appended when session registers in KV (server startup)
- **logout (graceful)**: Appended on server shutdown. Timestamp = last_active.
- **logout (TTL expiry)**: Appended by a **KV watcher** that observes key deletions. When a KV key expires or is deleted, the watcher appends a logout event using the session's `last_active` as the timestamp — NOT the TTL expiry moment. The 3-day TTL is a liveness grace period, not session duration. Actual session duration = `last_active - login_time`.

Example: engineer logs in at 9am, works until 11am (last_active=11am), closes laptop. KV key TTLs 3 days later. /last shows:
```
kai      tty1   kais-mbp       Feb 22 09:00 - 11:00  (02:00)
```

### KV Watcher

A background task (similar to `poll_inbox`) that watches the sessions KV bucket for delete events. On each delete:
1. Deserialize the deleted value to get the `UserSession` (NATS KV delete events include the last value)
2. Extract `last_active` as the logout timestamp
3. Append a logout `SessionEvent` to the wtmp stream

This ensures every login gets a matching logout, whether graceful or TTL-based — mirroring how Unix init writes the logout record to wtmp when a process exits.

### Retention Policy

| Resource | Retention | Value | Purpose |
|----------|-----------|-------|---------|
| KV sessions | TTL per key | 3 days | Liveness grace (laptop closed) |
| wtmp stream | `max_age` | 30 days | Historical session log |
| wtmp stream | `max_bytes` | TBD (10-50 MiB) | Size cap |

### /last Output

Reverse chronological. Cross-references wtmp (events) with utmp (KV, for "still logged in"):

```
/last

kai      tty1   kais-mbp       Feb 22 09:14   still logged in
eric     tty1   erics-mbp      Feb 22 08:45 - 09:30  (00:45)
kai:w1   tty2   kais-mbp       Feb 22 08:20 - 09:14  (00:54)
maya     tty1   mayas-mbp      Feb 22 08:15 - 08:22  (00:07)
kai      tty2   kais-mbp       Feb 22 08:00 - 08:20  (00:20)
```

### Filtering (matches Unix last flags)

- `/last` — default 10 entries
- `/last kai` — sessions for user kai
- `/last tty1` — sessions on tty1
- `/last kai tty2` — kai on tty2
- `/last -20` — last 20 entries

### Future: Teleport + Agent Teams visibility

With the Teleport bridge and Agent Teams bridge (see research/agentic-engineering-landscape-2026.md), `/last` shows the full picture:

```
kai         tty1     kais-mbp       Feb 22 09:14   still logged in
kai:remote  —        claude.ai      Feb 22 09:10   still logged in
eric        tty1     erics-mbp      Feb 22 08:45 - 09:30  (00:45)
kai:w1      tty2     kais-mbp       Feb 22 08:20 - 09:14  (00:54)
@ox         —        sageox.ai      Feb 22 08:00   still logged in
```

### What /last Is NOT

- Not message history (POP semantics unchanged, DES-006 stands)
- Not a task log (beads handles work tracking)
- Not a chat archive

It is the session ledger: who was here, when, for how long.

### Implementation Notes

- KV watcher needs access to the deleted value to extract `last_active`. NATS KV watch events for deletes include the last revision — verify this in nats.py client.
- The watcher should run on every connected biff server (idempotent — duplicate logout events for same session can be deduplicated by session key + event type).
- LocalRelay equivalent: append to a local wtmp JSONL file. Lower priority — LocalRelay is a testing harness.

### Priority

P3 — completes the BSD vocabulary triad (utmp/wtmp/lastlog → /who, /last, /finger) and is the natural foundation for the Teleport and Agent Teams bridge designs.

title: Enriched presence: host, directory, bead in /who
description:
## Problem

When multiple agents work in the same repository, they need to distinguish between two kinds of overlap:

1. **Logical overlap** (same bead) — bad regardless of machine. Prevented by seeing each other's /plan.
2. **Physical overlap** (same host + directory) — causes git working tree conflicts. Only matters when agents share the same filesystem.

Currently /who shows name, idle, status, and plan. It lacks the physical context needed to detect filesystem conflicts.

## Solution

### Enhanced Presence Data

Add to the NATS presence payload:
- `hostname` — machine identifier
- `pwd` — current working directory
- `git_root` — git repository root (if in a git repo)
- `branch` — current git branch

### Enhanced /who Output

```
▶  NAME    HOST       DIR                  PLAN
   @kai    macbook    ~/Coding/biff        biff-bf8: workflow toggle
   @eric   macbook    ~/Coding/biff        biff-bf9: enriched presence
                      ⚠ same host+dir
   @dana   server-2   ~/Coding/biff        biff-bf3: relay refactor
```

- Flag `⚠ same host+dir` when two sessions share hostname AND directory/git_root
- Same repo on different hosts = no flag (separate filesystems)

### /plan Shows Bead Context

When an agent sets /dotplan after claiming a bead, the plan text includes the bead ID and title. This is the cross-machine coordination layer — everyone sees what logical work each agent owns.

### Coordination via /write

When an agent detects a same-host+dir conflict via /who, it uses /write to directly coordinate with the other agent. The /write notification arrives as a push (tool name change notification mechanism), not passive inbox. AGENTS.md instructs agents to:
- Check /who when claiming work
- /write colliding agents to coordinate
- Create git worktrees when sharing a filesystem
- Act on /write notifications about workspace conflicts before next file operation

### Two Coordination Planes

| Plane | Scope | Tool | Detects | Action |
|-------|-------|------|---------|--------|
| Logical | Cross-machine | /plan + /who | Same bead claimed twice | Don't duplicate work |
| Physical | Same machine | /who (host+dir) + /write | Same working tree | Create worktree |

/wall stays clean for team announcements (PR created, merged, released). Physical coordination uses /who + /write between the specific agents involved.

## Implementation Notes

- Presence data published to NATS on session start and on branch switch
- hostname from `os.uname().nodename` or equivalent
- pwd from session environment
- git_root/branch from `git rev-parse` at publish time
- Collision detection is server-side: /who response includes flags
- Agents cannot read the status bar but DO receive tool name change notifications — this is how /write functions as push

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Presence payload includes hostname, pwd, git_root, branch
- [ ] /who displays host and directory columns
- [ ] /who flags same-host+dir overlaps
- [ ] /plan integrates with bead context (shows bead ID + title)
- [ ] /write notification serves as push coordination mechanism
- [ ] AGENTS.md (from biff-bf8) includes worktree guidance for physical conflicts
close_reason: Superseded. Core presence work (hostname/pwd in payload, HOST/DIR columns in /who) already built. Remaining items decomposed into focused beads: biff-5zq (plan bead expansion), biff-bf8 (AGENTS.md + workflow toggle), biff-9pl (collision detection, git_root/branch, /write coordination).

title: Tier 2b: CLI multi-user scenario tests via cli_session + local NATS
description:
## Problem

The CLI's cli_session() + biff.commands pure async functions create a
new testing surface between tier 2 (in-memory MCP) and tier 3b (full
NATS E2E). Two cli_session() instances share a local NATS server and
can exchange messages, wall broadcasts, and talk notifications — no
MCP protocol overhead, no stdin threads, no display concerns.

Current NATS relay coverage is 25% because tier 3b tests require
full MCP server infrastructure. cli_session() tests would exercise
real NATS paths at a fraction of the complexity.

## Design

New test directory: tests/test_cli_multi_user/

Fixtures:
- kai_and_eric: two cli_session() instances with local nats-server
- Reuses nats-server fixture from test_nats_e2e

Scenarios:
1. Presence: both appear in /who, each sees the other
2. Messaging: write → read (POP semantics)
3. Wall broadcast: post → read visibility
4. Plan visibility: plan → finger
5. Talk handshake: invite → accept → messages → mutual hangup
6. Session cleanup: exit → /who no longer shows session
7. wtmp: login/logout events in /last
8. mesg off: write still delivers to inbox

pytest mark: @pytest.mark.nats (reuses existing local NATS marker)

title: Docker image for self-hosted NATS relay
description:
## Problem

Self-hosting a NATS relay for biff requires NATS expertise: install
nats-server, enable JetStream, configure streams. The .biff relay_url
already supports pointing at a custom server, but there's no turnkey
way to get one running.

## Design

A pre-configured Docker image: docker run punt-labs/biff-relay

Contents:
- nats-server with JetStream enabled
- Health check endpoint
- Documentation for .biff configuration

The image does NOT provision streams — biff's NatsRelay creates
shared streams (DES-016) on first connection. The Docker image
just needs to run nats-server -js on a known port.

## Usage

\`\`\`bash
docker run -d -p 4222:4222 punt-labs/biff-relay
\`\`\`

Then in .biff:
\`\`\`toml
[relay]
url = "nats://your-server:4222"
\`\`\`

## What this replaces

The demo relay (tls://connect.ngs.global) is fine for getting started
but shares namespace with all biff users. Self-hosted gives teams
full isolation with zero dependency on Synadia Cloud.

## Relationship to other beads

- biff-bv5 (hosted relay service): the paid managed tier. This bead
  is the self-hosted free tier.
- biff-3pn (per-repo credentials): relevant for hosted, not self-hosted
  (self-hosted teams manage their own NATS auth).

title: SessionStart collision detection: worktree advisory when same-host+dir overlap
description:
## Problem

Multiple agents starting in the same directory can interfere with each other (file edits, git operations). Biff has all the primitives (/who with host+dir, /plan, worktree-scoped hints) but nothing calls them automatically on session start.

## Solution

1. **SessionStart hook**: Check /who for same-host + same-dir + different-tty sessions. If found, inject a worktree advisory into additionalContext.
2. **WorktreeCreate hook**: Update session pwd to the new worktree path so /who reflects isolation and the collision flag clears.
3. No new MCP tools or commands needed — just hook logic + session update path.

## Success Criteria

Two agents in the same directory: the second one gets a collision warning and creates a worktree. /who shows them in separate directories. Files never conflict.

## Parent Epic

biff-9pl (Agentic coordination)
close_reason: Merged PR #91: SessionStart collision detection
